You are a hostile mathematical referee. Audit these abstract claims from a Farey discrepancy paper for errors, overstatements, or unjustified assertions. Mark each claim: VALID / OVERSTATED / WRONG / NEEDS-QUALIFICATION. Check: (1) are Perron/phase-lock claims marked conditional on GRH? (2) Is 0.07 rad phase error correctly derived? (3) Is computational vs analytical distinction clear? (4) Any missing caveats?

\begin{abstract}
Per-step Farey discrepancy $\Delta W(N) = W(N-1) - W(N)$ has never been
studied.  It reveals structure invisible to the classical cumulative
discrepancy~$W(N)$, and led to the following discoveries:
\begin{enumerate}[(i)]
  \item An exact four-term algebraic decomposition of $\Delta W(p)$ for
    every prime~$p$ (Theorem~\ref{thm:four-term}), reducing the sign
    of~$\Delta W$ to an explicit number-theoretic sum $T(N)$.
  \item A regression identity $B'/C' = \alpha + \rho$ (Theorem~\ref{thm:regression}),
    proved via permutation invariance of coprime residues.
  \item Computational verification that $\Delta W(p) < 0$ for all
    4{,}617 primes $p \le 10^5$ with $M(p) \le -3$, and discovery of the
    first counterexample at $p = 243{,}799$.
  \item A Perron integral formula for $T(N)$ (Theorem~\ref{thm:perron},
    conditional on GRH), expressing $T(N)$ as $-2\log N + C_0
    + \sum_\rho c_\rho N^\rho + O(1)$ with computable residues~$c_\rho$.
  \item A phase-lock theorem and Chebyshev bias
    (Theorem~\ref{thm:phase-lock}, conditional on GRH+LI):
    the sign of $\Delta W(p)$ is controlled by
    $\gamma_1 \log p \bmod 2\pi$, where $\gamma_1 = 14.134\ldots$ is the
    first zeta zero.  The predicted phase matches observation to
    $0.07$~radians.
  \item A spectral decomposition indexed by Dirichlet characters
    (Section~\ref{sec:l-function}): each twisted summatory function~$T_\chi$
    oscillates at frequencies determined by zeros of~$L(s,\chi)$.
  \item A null test (Section~\ref{sec:ordering}): the Stern--Brocot tree
    enumerates the same rationals but produces no spectral signature,
    confirming that the zeta-zero oscillation is a property of the
    \emph{arithmetic ordering by denominator}, not of the rationals
    themselves.
  \item Unconditional results (Theorem~\ref{thm:unconditional}):
    $T(N) = \Omega_\pm(\sqrt{N})$ via Ingham's method, giving
    infinitely many primes with $\Delta W > 0$ and infinitely many
    with $\Delta W < 0$.
\end{enumerate}
\end{abstract}

./experiments/AMR_PAPER_DRAFT.md:416:**Non-tensor-product 3D Farey meshes.** The Stern--Brocot tree naturally extends to higher-dimensional mediant constructions via continued fractions in multiple variables. A Farey--Delaunay construction---where new nodes are placed at mediant positions but triangulated using Delaunay criteria---could combine the injection principle's zero-cascading guarantee with the geometric optimality of Delaunay meshes. This remains an open research problem.

    50	
    51	\begin{document}
    52	
    53	\maketitle
    54	
    55	\begin{abstract}
    56	Per-step Farey discrepancy $\Delta W(N) = W(N-1) - W(N)$ has never been
    57	studied.  It reveals structure invisible to the classical cumulative
    58	discrepancy~$W(N)$, and led to the following discoveries:
    59	\begin{enumerate}[(i)]
    60	  \item An exact four-term algebraic decomposition of $\Delta W(p)$ for
    61	    every prime~$p$ (Theorem~\ref{thm:four-term}), reducing the sign
    62	    of~$\Delta W$ to an explicit number-theoretic sum $T(N)$.
    63	  \item A regression identity $B'/C' = \alpha + \rho$ (Theorem~\ref{thm:regression}),
    64	    proved via permutation invariance of coprime residues.
    65	  \item Computational verification that $\Delta W(p) < 0$ for all
    66	    4{,}617 primes $p \le 10^5$ with $M(p) \le -3$, and discovery of the
    67	    first counterexample at $p = 243{,}799$.
    68	  \item A Perron integral formula for $T(N)$ (Theorem~\ref{thm:perron},
    69	    conditional on GRH), expressing $T(N)$ as $-2\log N + C_0
    70	    + \sum_\rho c_\rho N^\rho + O(1)$ with computable residues~$c_\rho$.
    71	  \item A phase-lock theorem and Chebyshev bias
    72	    (Theorem~\ref{thm:phase-lock}, conditional on GRH+LI):
    73	    the sign of $\Delta W(p)$ is controlled by
    74	    $\gamma_1 \log p \bmod 2\pi$, where $\gamma_1 = 14.134\ldots$ is the
    75	    first zeta zero.  The predicted phase matches observation to
    76	    $0.07$~radians.
    77	  \item A spectral decomposition indexed by Dirichlet characters
    78	    (Section~\ref{sec:l-function}): each twisted summatory function~$T_\chi$
    79	    oscillates at frequencies determined by zeros of~$L(s,\chi)$.
    80	  \item A null test (Section~\ref{sec:ordering}): the Stern--Brocot tree
    81	    enumerates the same rationals but produces no spectral signature,
    82	    confirming that the zeta-zero oscillation is a property of the
    83	    \emph{arithmetic ordering by denominator}, not of the rationals
    84	    themselves.
    85	  \item Unconditional results (Theorem~\ref{thm:unconditional}):
    86	    $T(N) = \Omega_\pm(\sqrt{N})$ via Ingham's method, giving
    87	    infinitely many primes with $\Delta W > 0$ and infinitely many
    88	    with $\Delta W < 0$.
    89	\end{enumerate}
    90	\end{abstract}
    91	
    92	\medskip
    93	\noindent\textbf{Keywords:} Farey sequence, per-step discrepancy,
    94	Mertens function, Chebyshev bias, zeta zeros, Dirichlet $L$-functions,
    95	Stern--Brocot tree.

 succeeded in 0ms:
   320	\begin{theorem}[Four-term decomposition]\label{thm:four-term}
   321	For every prime $p \ge 5$:
   322	\begin{equation}\label{eq:four-term}
   323	  n'^2 \Delta W(p) = A - B - C - D,
   324	\end{equation}
   325	where:
   326	\begin{align}
   327	  A &= \Bigl(\frac{n'^2}{n^2} - 1\Bigr)
   328	    \sum_{f \in \F_N} D_{\F_N}(f)^2
   329	    && \text{(dilution gain),} \\
   330	  B &= 2\sum_{f \in F} D_{\F_N}(f)\,\delta(f)
   331	    && \text{(cross term),} \\
   332	  C &= \sum_{f \in F} \delta(f)^2
   333	    && \text{(shift-squared cost),} \\
   334	  D &= \sum_{k=1}^{p-1} D_{\F_p}(k/p)^2
   335	    && \text{(new-fraction cost).}
   336	\end{align}
   337	Moreover, $|D/A - 1| = O(1/p^2)$ as $p \to \infty$.
   338	\end{theorem}
   339	
   340	\begin{proof}
   341	Expanding $n'^2 W(p) = \sum_{f \in \F_p} D_{\F_p}(f)^2$ via the
   342	displacement--shift identity:
   343	\begin{align*}
   344	  n'^2 W(p) &= \sum_{f \in \F_N} (D_{\F_N}(f) + \delta(f))^2
   345	    + \sum_{k=1}^{p-1} D_{\F_p}(k/p)^2 \\
   346	  &= n^2 W(N) + B + C + D.
   347	\end{align*}
   348	Then $n'^2 \Delta W(p) = n'^2 W(N) - n'^2 W(p)
   349	= (n'^2 - n^2)W(N) - B - C - D = A - B - C - D$.
   350	
   351	For $D/A \to 1$: writing the new-fraction ranks as
   352	$D_{\F_p}(k/p) = k - n'(k/p)$, one has $D = \Theta(p^3)$ and
   353	$A = \Theta(p^3)$, with $|D/A - 1| = O(1/p^2)$ from matching the
   354	leading terms.
   355	\end{proof}
   356	
   357	Since $D \approx A$, the sign of $\Delta W(p)$ is controlled by
   358	$B + C$: positive $B + C$ means the cross and shift terms absorb
   359	the dilution-cost difference, giving $\Delta W < 0$ (regularity
   360	worsens).
   361	
   362	
   363	
   364	%% ================================================================
   365	%% SECTION 3: THE REGRESSION IDENTITY
   366	%% ================================================================
   367	\section{The Regression Identity $B'/C' = \alpha + \rho$}
   368	\label{sec:regression}
   369	
   370	\begin{lemma}[Shift residual vanishing]\label{lem:shift-vanish}
   371	For each denominator $b \in \{2, \ldots, N\}$:
   372	\[
   373	  \sum_{\substack{a=1 \\ \gcd(a,b)=1}}^{b-1} \delta(a/b) = 0.
   374	\]
   375	\end{lemma}
   376	
   377	\begin{proof}
   378	Since $p$ is prime and $b < p$, the map $a \mapsto pa \bmod b$ is a
   379	permutation of the coprime residues.  Hence
   380	$\sum_a \delta(a/b) = \frac{1}{b}[\sum_a a - \sum_a a] = 0$.
   381	\end{proof}
   382	
   383	\begin{corollary}\label{cor:sum-delta-zero}
   384	$\sum_{f \in F} \delta(f) = 0$.
   385	\end{corollary}
   386	
   387	\begin{lemma}[Permutation square-sum identity]\label{lem:perm-sq}
   388	$\sum_{f \in F} f \cdot \delta(f) = C'/2$.
   389	\end{lemma}
   390	
   391	\begin{proof}
   392	Write $\delta(f) = f - \{pf\}$.  Then
   393	$f\,\delta(f) - \delta(f)^2/2 = (f^2 - \{pf\}^2)/2$.
   394	For each fixed~$b$, the permutation $a \mapsto pa \bmod b$ gives
   395	$\sum_a (a/b)^2 = \sum_a ((pa \bmod b)/b)^2$.  Summing over all~$b$
   396	yields $\sum f\,\delta = C'/2$.
   397	\end{proof}
   398	
   399	\begin{theorem}[Regression identity]\label{thm:regression}
   400	For every prime $p \ge 5$:
   401	\begin{equation}\label{eq:regression}
   402	  \frac{B'}{C'} = \alpha + \rho,
   403	\end{equation}
   404	where $\alpha = \Cov(D,f)/\Var(f)$ and
   405	$\rho = 2\sum D_{\mathrm{err}}(f)\,\delta(f)/C'$.
   406	\end{theorem}
   407	
   408	\begin{proof}
   409	Decompose $D(f) = \bar{D} + \alpha(f - 1/2) + D_{\mathrm{err}}(f)$.  Then:
   410	\[
   411	  B' = 2\bar{D}\underbrace{\sum\delta}_{=\,0}
   412	  + 2\alpha\underbrace{\sum(f - \tfrac{1}{2})\delta}_{=\,C'/2}
   413	  + 2\sum D_{\mathrm{err}}\,\delta
   414	  = \alpha C' + \rho C'.  \qedhere
   415	\]
   416	\end{proof}
   417	
   418	\begin{remark}
   419	This identity is purely algebraic: no approximations, no asymptotics.
   420	It holds exactly for every prime $p \ge 5$.  The connection to~$T(N)$
   421	comes through the classical relation $\alpha \approx 1 - T(N)$ (see
   422	Theorem~\ref{thm:alpha-formula}), which links the OLS slope to the
   423	Mertens function at multiple scales.
   424	\end{remark}
   425	
   426	\begin{theorem}[Alpha formula]\label{thm:alpha-formula}
   427	For prime~$p$ with $M(p-1) = -2$:
   428	\[
   429	  \alpha = 1 - T(N) + O(1/N),
   430	\]

   542	%% ================================================================
   543	%% SECTION 5: THE PERRON INTEGRAL FOR T(N)
   544	%% ================================================================
   545	\section{The Perron Integral for $T(N)$}\label{sec:perron}
   546	
   547	\begin{theorem}[Conditional on GRH]\label{thm:perron}
   548	Assume the Generalized Riemann Hypothesis.  Then for $M(N) = -2$:
   549	\begin{equation}\label{eq:perron}
   550	  T(N) = -2\log N + C_0 + \sum_{\rho} c_\rho\, N^\rho + O(1),
   551	\end{equation}
   552	where $C_0 = 2(1 - \gamma + \log 2\pi) = 4.521\ldots$,
   553	$c_\rho = \zeta(\rho+1)/(\rho\,\zeta'(\rho))$,
   554	and the sum is over nontrivial zeros~$\rho$ of $\zeta(s)$.
   555	\end{theorem}
   556	
   557	\begin{proof}
   558	Define $F(N) = \sum_{m=1}^{N} M(\lfloor N/m \rfloor)/m$, so
   559	$T(N) = F(N) - M(N)$.  The Perron integral representation is
   560	\begin{equation}\label{eq:perron-integral}
   561	  F(N) = \frac{1}{2\pi i} \int_{(c)} N^s \cdot
   562	  \frac{\zeta(s+1)}{s\,\zeta(s)}\,ds, \qquad c > 1,
   563	\end{equation}
   564	using the Dirichlet series identities: $\mu(n)$ has generating
   565	function $1/\zeta(s)$, the harmonic weight $1/n$ has generating
   566	function $\zeta(s+1)$, and the Perron formula
   567	$\frac{1}{2\pi i}\int_{(c)} x^s/s\,ds = \mathbf{1}_{x > 1}$.
   568	
   569	The integrand $G(s) = N^s\,\zeta(s+1)/(s\,\zeta(s))$ has:
   570	\begin{enumerate}[(i)]
   571	  \item A double pole at $s = 0$ (from $1/s$ and the pole of
   572	    $\zeta(s+1)$);
   573	  \item Simple poles at $s = \rho$ for each nontrivial zero of
   574	    $\zeta(s)$;
   575	  \item Trivial-zero poles at $s = -2k$ ($k = 1, 2, \ldots$),
   576	    contributing $O(1)$.
   577	\end{enumerate}
   578	
   579	\textbf{Residue at $s = 0$.}  Using the Laurent expansions
   580	$N^s = 1 + s\log N + \cdots$,
   581	$\zeta(s+1) = 1/s + \gamma + \cdots$,
   582	$1/\zeta(s) = -2 + 2\log(2\pi)\cdot s + \cdots$
   583	(since $\zeta(0) = -1/2$):
   584	\[
   585	  \Res_{s=0} G(s) = -2(\log N + \gamma) + 2\log 2\pi
   586	  = -2\log N + 2(\log 2\pi - \gamma).
   587	\]
   588	
   589	\textbf{Residue at $s = \rho$.}
   590	$\Res_{s=\rho} G(s) = N^\rho \cdot \zeta(\rho+1)/(\rho\,\zeta'(\rho))
   591	= c_\rho\, N^\rho$.
   592	
   593	Shifting the contour to $\operatorname{Re}(s) = -1 - \varepsilon$
   594	(justified under GRH):
   595	\[
   596	  F(N) = -2\log N + 2(\log 2\pi - \gamma)
   597	  + \sum_\rho c_\rho\, N^\rho + O(1).
   598	\]
   599	Since $T(N) = F(N) - M(N)$ and $M(N) = -2$, we obtain~\eqref{eq:perron}.
   600	\end{proof}
   601	
   602	\subsection{Computed coefficients}
   603	
   604	Writing $\rho_k = 1/2 + i\gamma_k$ (assuming RH):
   605	
   606	\begin{center}
   607	\begin{tabular}{ccccc}
   608	\toprule
   609	$k$ & $\gamma_k$ & $|c_k|$ & $\arg(c_k)$ & Period $2\pi/\gamma_k$ \\
   610	\midrule
   611	1 & 14.1347 & 0.04853 & $-1.602$ & 0.445 \\
   612	2 & 21.0220 & 0.02778 & $-1.487$ & 0.299 \\
   613	3 & 25.0109 & 0.02170 & $-1.668$ & 0.251 \\
   614	4 & 30.4249 & 0.01704 & $-1.337$ & 0.207 \\
   615	5 & 32.9351 & 0.01526 & $-1.799$ & 0.191 \\
   616	\bottomrule
   617	\end{tabular}
   618	\end{center}
   619	
   620	Computed with \texttt{mpmath} at 30-digit precision.  The dominant
   621	oscillatory term is:
   622	\begin{equation}\label{eq:dominant-osc}
   623	  T(N) \approx -2\log N + 4.52
   624	  + 0.0971\sqrt{N}\cos(14.1347\log N - 1.602) + \cdots
   625	\end{equation}
   626	The oscillatory amplitude $O(\sqrt{N})$ eventually dominates the
   627	drift $-2\log N$, explaining why counterexamples emerge.
   628

   636	%% ================================================================
   637	%% SECTION 6: PHASE-LOCK THEOREM AND CHEBYSHEV BIAS
   638	%% ================================================================
   639	\section{Phase-Lock Theorem and Chebyshev Bias}\label{sec:phaselock}
   640	
   641	\begin{theorem}[Phase-lock and Chebyshev bias; conditional on GRH+LI]
   642	\label{thm:phase-lock}
   643	Assume GRH and the Linear Independence hypothesis
   644	($\gamma_1, \gamma_2, \ldots$ are linearly independent over~$\Q$).
   645	Then:
   646	
   647	\begin{enumerate}[\upshape(a)]
   648	  \item The logarithmic density
   649	  \[
   650	    \delta = \lim_{X \to \infty} \frac{1}{\log X}
   651	    \int_2^X \mathbf{1}_{T(t) > 0}\,\frac{dt}{t}
   652	  \]
   653	  exists and equals~$1/2$.
   654	
   655	  \item Among $N$ with $T(N) > 0$, the circular mean of
   656	  $\theta(N) = \gamma_1 \log N \bmod 2\pi$ concentrates at the
   657	  phase $\gamma_1 \log 2 - \arg(d_1) \pmod{2\pi}$, where
   658	  $d_1 = 1/(\rho_1\,\zeta'(\rho_1))$.
   659	
   660	  \item The finite-$N$ bias is $O(\log N / \sqrt{N})$.
   661	\end{enumerate}
   662	\end{theorem}
   663	
   664	\begin{proof}
   665	By Theorem~\ref{thm:perron}, $T(N)/\sqrt{N}$ converges in distribution
   666	(under logarithmic measure) to
   667	\[
   668	  Y = \sum_{\gamma_k > 0} 2|c_k|\cos(\theta_k + \arg c_k),
   669	\]
   670	where $\theta_k$ are independent uniform on $[0, 2\pi)$ (this is the
   671	Rubinstein--Sarnak framework~\cite{RubinsteinSarnak1994}, made rigorous
   672	under GRH+LI).
   673	
   674	\textbf{(a)} Each cosine term is symmetric about zero, so $Y$ has a
   675	symmetric distribution.  The condition $T(N) > 0$ becomes
   676	$Y > (2\log N - C_0)/\sqrt{N} \to 0$, so the density equals
   677	$\Pr(Y > 0) = 1/2$.
   678	
   679	\textbf{(b)} The $M(N/2)/2$ term dominates $T(N)$ (empirical
   680	correlation $r = 0.95$).  The Mertens function oscillates as
   681	$M(x) \sim 2\operatorname{Re}[d_1 x^{\rho_1}]$, where
   682	$|d_1| = 0.0891$, $\arg(d_1) = -1.693$.  Setting $x = N/2$:
   683	$M(N/2) > 0$ when
   684	$\gamma_1 \log N \approx \gamma_1 \log 2 - \arg(d_1) = 5.208$.
   685	
   686	\textbf{(c)} The effective threshold
   687	$\delta(N) = (2\log N - C_0)/\sqrt{N}$ gives
   688	$\Pr(T > 0) = 1/2 - O(\delta(N)/\sigma)$ where
   689	$\sigma^2 = \sum_k 2|c_k|^2 = 0.01018$.
   690	\end{proof}
   691	
   692	\subsection{Computational verification}
   693	
   694	Among the 922 primes $p \le 10^7$ with $M(p) = -3$:
   695	
   696	\begin{center}
   697	\begin{tabular}{lcc}
   698	\toprule
   699	Quantity & Observed & Predicted \\
   700	\midrule
   701	Circular mean of $\gamma_1 \log p$ for $T > 0$ & 5.28 & 5.208 \\
   702	Resultant length $R$ for $T > 0$ & 0.77 & --- \\
   703	Resultant length $R$ for $T < 0$ & 0.13 & --- \\
   704	Fraction $T > 0$ in $[10^6, 10^7)$ & 0.462 & 0.47 \\
   705	\bottomrule
   706	\end{tabular}
   707	\end{center}
   708	
   709	The phase match to $0.07$ radians ($1.1\%$) is the sharpest confirmation.
   710	The resultant length $R = 0.77$ for the $T > 0$ primes indicates
   711	extremely strong phase concentration, while $R = 0.13$ for $T < 0$
   712	indicates near-uniform distribution.
   713	
   714	%% FIGURE: Phase-lock scatter plot.  Polar plot of gamma_1 * log(p) mod 2*pi
   715	%% for each of the 922 primes.  T > 0 primes shown as filled dots (concentrated
   716	%% in the arc [4.2, 5.8]), T < 0 primes as open circles (uniformly distributed).
   717	%% Mark the predicted phase 5.208 with a radial line.
   718	
   719	\subsection{Extreme clustering}
   720	
   721	The 247 primes with $T(N) > 0$ form only 16 runs, with lengths up to
   722	81 consecutive $M(p) = -3$ primes.  This reflects the slow
   723	$\gamma_1$-oscillation: when $\gamma_1 \log N$ is in the favorable
   724	phase window $[4.2, 5.8]$, all nearby primes share $T > 0$.  The
   725	phase window width in $N$ is $N \cdot 1.6/\gamma_1 \approx 0.11N$,
   726	explaining why runs of dozens of primes are typical.
   727	
   728	\subsection{Comparison with classical Chebyshev bias}
   729	
   730	In the classical prime race $\pi(x;4,3) > \pi(x;4,1)$,
   731	Rubinstein--Sarnak showed the logarithmic density of the bias is
   732	$0.9959\ldots$, driven by a constant offset $1/(2\sqrt{x})$ from
   733	the exceptional prime $p = 2$.  Our problem differs:
   734	\begin{itemize}
   735	  \item The offset $-2\log N/\sqrt{N}$ \emph{vanishes} as $N \to \infty$.
   736	  \item The limiting density is $1/2$ (no permanent bias), unlike
   737	    $0.9959$.
   738	  \item The bias is a \emph{Farey regularity} question, not a
   739	    prime-counting question.
   740	\end{itemize}
   741

   745	%% SECTION 7: L-FUNCTION SPECTRAL DECOMPOSITION
   746	%% ================================================================
   747	\section{$L$-Function Spectral Decomposition}\label{sec:l-function}
   748	
   749	\subsection{Twisted summatory functions}
   750	
   751	For a Dirichlet character $\chi \bmod q$, define the twisted Mertens
   752	function $M_\chi(x) = \sum_{k \le x} \mu(k)\chi(k)$ and the twisted
   753	summatory function
   754	\begin{equation}\label{eq:T-chi}
   755	  T_\chi(N) = \sum_{m=2}^{N} \frac{M_\chi(\lfloor N/m \rfloor)}{m}.
   756	\end{equation}
   757	When $\chi = \chi_0$ (the trivial character mod~1), this
   758	reduces to the original~$T(N)$.
   759	
   760	\begin{theorem}[Spectral decomposition principle]\label{thm:spectral-decomp}

   853	\section{The Ordering Principle: Farey vs.\ Stern--Brocot}
   854	\label{sec:ordering}
   855	
   856	\subsection{The Stern--Brocot tree}
   857	
   858	The Stern--Brocot tree generates all positive rationals via mediant
   859	insertion.  Restricted to $[0,1]$, level~$n$ has $2^n$ new fractions,
   860	and the cumulative set $S_n$ has $2^n + 1$ elements.  The per-level
   861	discrepancy is
   862	$\Delta W_{\mathrm{SB}}(n) = W(S_n) - W(S_{n-1})$.
   863	
   864	\subsection{Results: no spectral structure}
   865	
   866	\begin{center}
   867	\begin{tabular}{lcc}
   868	\toprule
   869	Property & Stern--Brocot & Farey \\
   870	\midrule
   871	$\Delta W$ behavior & Monotone growth & Oscillation \\
   872	Sign changes (first 21 steps) & 1 & 14 \\
   873	Growth & $\Delta W \sim C \cdot 2^n$ & $\Delta W \sim O(1/N)$ \\
   874	Spectral content & None (pure geometric) & Locked to $\zeta$ zeros \\
   875	Governing function & Minkowski $?(x)$ & Riemann $\zeta$ / Mertens \\
   876	Ordering principle & Tree depth (CF structure) & Denominator bound \\
   877	\bottomrule
   878	\end{tabular}
   879	\end{center}
   880	
   881	The Stern--Brocot per-level discrepancy satisfies
   882	$\Delta W_{\mathrm{SB}}(n) / 2^{n-1} \to 0.00789\ldots$
   883	(the integral of $(?(x) - x)^2$ against Lebesgue measure,
   884	where~$?$ is Minkowski's question mark function), with ratio
   885	converging to exactly~$2$ and \emph{zero oscillatory content}.
   886	
   887	\subsection{The Calkin--Wilf tree is identical}
   888	
   889	The cumulative sets $S_n$ are the same for both trees at every level
   890	(verified exactly to level~11, numerically to level~22).  The
   891	discrepancies are identical:
   892	$\Delta W_{\mathrm{CW}} = \Delta W_{\mathrm{SB}}$ exactly.
   893	
   894	\subsection{Interpretation}
   895	
   896	The absence of spectral structure in Stern--Brocot discrepancy confirms
   897	that the zeta-zero oscillation in Farey $\Delta W$ is a consequence of
   898	the \emph{arithmetic ordering by denominator}, not an intrinsic property
   899	of the rational numbers.  The same rationals, ordered by tree depth,
   900	produce qualitatively different dynamics:
   901	\begin{itemize}
   902	  \item \textbf{Farey (denominator ordering):} Step-by-step inclusion
   903	    introduces number-theoretic correlations (Euler totient, M\"obius
   904	    function) that encode zeta zeros in the oscillation of $\Delta W$.
   905	  \item \textbf{Stern--Brocot (tree ordering):} Mediant-based insertion
   906	    has a purely geometric doubling structure.  The discrepancy is
   907	    governed by Minkowski's~$?(x)$, a singular function with no
   908	    connection to $\zeta$.
   909	\end{itemize}
   910	
   911	\emph{Different orderings of the same rationals reveal different
   912	spectral data---or none at all.  The Farey ordering by denominator is
   913	special because it engages multiplicative number theory, making zeta
   914	zeros visible.}
   915	
   916	%% FIGURE: Side-by-side comparison.
   917	%% Left panel: Farey DeltaW(N) for N = 2, ..., 200 showing oscillation.
   918	%% Right panel: Stern-Brocot DeltaW(n) for n = 1, ..., 22 showing
   919	%% monotone exponential growth.  Mark the ratio 2.000 on the right panel.

   923	%% ================================================================
   924	%% SECTION 9: UNCONDITIONAL RESULTS
   925	%% ================================================================
   926	\section{Unconditional Results}\label{sec:unconditional}
   927	
   928	The results of Sections~\ref{sec:perron}--\ref{sec:phaselock} are
   929	conditional on GRH (and LI for the density statement).  We now give
   930	what can be proved without any unproven hypothesis.
   931	
   932	\begin{theorem}[Unconditional oscillation]\label{thm:unconditional}
   933	$T(N) = \Omega_\pm(\sqrt{N})$.  That is:
   934	\[
   935	  \limsup_{N \to \infty} \frac{T(N)}{\sqrt{N}} > 0, \qquad
   936	  \liminf_{N \to \infty} \frac{T(N)}{\sqrt{N}} < 0.
   937	\]
   938	\end{theorem}
   939	
   940	\begin{proof}
   941	Adapting the method of Ingham~\cite{Ingham1942}.  The generating
   942	function of $T(N)$ (excluding the $m = 1$ term) corresponds to the
   943	Dirichlet series $[\zeta(s+1)/(s\,\zeta(s))] - [1/\zeta(s)]
   944	= [\zeta(s+1)/s - 1]/\zeta(s)$.
   945	
   946	Near a zero $\rho = 1/2 + i\gamma$ of $\zeta$ on the critical line
   947	(guaranteed to exist by Hardy's theorem~\cite{Hardy1914}), the
   948	factor $[\zeta(\rho+1)/\rho - 1]$ is a nonzero constant, so this
   949	Dirichlet series has a singularity at $s = \rho$.
   950	
   951	Suppose for contradiction that $T(N) < C\sqrt{N}$ for all~$N$.  Then
   952	the Dirichlet series converges for $\operatorname{Re}(s) > 1/2$,
   953	forcing analyticity there.  But zeta has zeros on
   954	$\operatorname{Re}(s) = 1/2$ (a positive proportion, by
   955	Selberg~\cite{Selberg1942}), giving singularities at these points.
   956	Contradiction.
   957	
   958	The $\Omega_-$ direction follows by the same argument applied
   959	to~$-T(N)$.
   960	\end{proof}
   961	
   962	\begin{corollary}\label{cor:infinitely-many}
   963	Among primes $p$ with $M(p) = -3$:
   964	\begin{enumerate}[\upshape(a)]
   965	  \item Infinitely many have $\Delta W(p) > 0$
   966	    \textup{(from the $\Omega_+$ result)};
   967	  \item Infinitely many have $\Delta W(p) < 0$
   968	    \textup{(from the Sign Theorem to $10^5$)}.
   969	\end{enumerate}
   970	The lower logarithmic density of each set is positive.

   960	\end{proof}
   961	
   962	\begin{corollary}\label{cor:infinitely-many}
   963	Among primes $p$ with $M(p) = -3$:
   964	\begin{enumerate}[\upshape(a)]
   965	  \item Infinitely many have $\Delta W(p) > 0$
   966	    \textup{(from the $\Omega_+$ result)};
   967	  \item Infinitely many have $\Delta W(p) < 0$
   968	    \textup{(from the Sign Theorem to $10^5$)}.
   969	\end{enumerate}
   970	The lower logarithmic density of each set is positive.
   971	\end{corollary}
   972	
   973	\begin{theorem}[Effective upper bound]\label{thm:effective-bound}
   974	There exists an absolute constant $c > 0$ \textup{(ineffective)} such
   975	that
   976	\[
   977	  |T(N)| \le N \cdot \exp\!\bigl(-c\,(\log N)^{3/5}
   978	  (\log\log N)^{-1/5}\bigr)
   979	\]
   980	for all sufficiently large~$N$.
   981	\end{theorem}
   982	
   983	\begin{proof}
   984	Apply the Walfisz bound $|M(x)| \le x\,\exp(-c(\log x)^{3/5}
   985	(\log\log x)^{-1/5})$ to each term of
   986	$T(N) = \sum_{m=2}^{N} M(\lfloor N/m \rfloor)/m$, splitting at
   987	$m = \sqrt{N}$.  The low-$m$ terms contribute at most
   988	$O(N\,\exp(-c'(\log N)^{3/5}/(\log\log N)^{1/5}))$; the high-$m$
   989	terms contribute $O(\sqrt{N}\log N)$, which is dominated.
   990	\end{proof}
   991	
   992	\begin{remark}
   993	The gap between the oscillation result ($\Omega_\pm(\sqrt{N})$)
   994	and the upper bound ($O(N/\exp(\cdots))$) is enormous.  Under GRH,
   995	$|T(N)| = O(\sqrt{N}\log^2 N)$, nearly matching the oscillation.
   996	Closing this gap unconditionally would constitute major progress on
   997	zeta-zero distribution.
   998	\end{remark}
   999	
  1000	\subsection{Partial explicit formula without GRH}
  1001	
  1002	\begin{proposition}[Unconditional]\label{prop:partial-explicit}
  1003	For any $\sigma \in (1/2, 1)$ and $T > 0$:
  1004	\[
  1005	  F(N) = -2\log N + C_0
  1006	  + \!\!\!\sum_{\substack{\rho :\, \operatorname{Re}(\rho) > \sigma \\
  1007	  |\operatorname{Im}(\rho)| < T}} \!\!\! c_\rho\,N^\rho
  1008	  + O\!\left(\frac{N^\sigma \log^2 T}{T}\right)
  1009	  + O(N\,e^{-c\sqrt{\log N}}).
  1010	\]
  1011	\end{proposition}
  1012	
  1013	Using the Ingham zero-density estimate, the contribution from
  1014	off-line zeros (if any exist) is at most $O(N^{1/2+\varepsilon})$,
  1015	so the on-line zeros dominate regardless.
  1016

  1020	%% SECTION 10: MULTI-SCALE STRUCTURE

  1022	\section{Multi-Scale Decomposition of $T(N)$}\label{sec:multiscale}
  1023	
  1024	\subsection{Scale bands}
  1025	
  1026	Decompose $T(N) = T_{\mathrm{low}} + T_{\mathrm{mid}} + T_{\mathrm{high}}$
  1027	where:
  1028	\begin{align*}
  1029	  T_{\mathrm{low}}(N) &= \sum_{m=2}^{10}
  1030	    \frac{M(\lfloor N/m \rfloor)}{m}
  1031	    & &\text{(large scales: $N/2$ to $N/10$),} \\
  1032	  T_{\mathrm{mid}}(N) &= \sum_{m=11}^{\sqrt{N}}
  1033	    \frac{M(\lfloor N/m \rfloor)}{m}
  1034	    & &\text{(medium scales),} \\
  1035	  T_{\mathrm{high}}(N) &= \sum_{m > \sqrt{N}}^{N}

   760	\begin{theorem}[Spectral decomposition principle]\label{thm:spectral-decomp}
   761	Per-step Farey discrepancy admits a spectral decomposition indexed by
   762	Dirichlet characters, where each component $T_\chi(N)$ oscillates at
   763	frequencies determined by the nontrivial zeros of~$L(s,\chi)$.
   764	
   765	More precisely, the Perron integral representation
   766	\begin{equation}\label{eq:perron-chi}
   767	  T_\chi(N) + M_\chi(N)
   768	  = \frac{1}{2\pi i}\int_{(c)} N^s \cdot
   769	  \frac{L(s{+}1,\chi)}{s\,L(s,\chi)}\,ds
   770	\end{equation}
   771	has poles at the nontrivial zeros of $L(s,\chi)$, with residues
   772	\begin{equation}\label{eq:residue-chi}
   773	  c_{\rho,\chi} = \frac{L(\rho{+}1,\chi)}{\rho\, L'(\rho,\chi)}
   774	  \cdot N^\rho.
   775	\end{equation}
   776	\end{theorem}
   777	
   778	\subsection{Computational evidence}
   779	
   780	\begin{center}
   781	\begin{tabular}{lccc}
   782	\toprule
   783	Test & $R(T{>}0)$ & $\sigma$ & Verdict \\
   784	\midrule
   785	$T_{\mathrm{plain}}$ at $\gamma_1(\zeta) = 14.13$ & 0.849 & 4.1 & Strong \\
   786	$T_{\chi_{-4}}$ at $\gamma_1(L,\chi_{-4}) = 6.02$ & 0.156 & 3.2 & Clear \\
   787	$T_{\chi_3}$ at $\gamma_1(L,\chi_3) = 8.04$ & 0.276 & 5.1 & Very strong \\
   788	$T_{\mathrm{plain}}$ at $\gamma_2(\zeta) = 21.02$ & 0.547 & 2.6 & Moderate \\
   789	\midrule
   790	\multicolumn{4}{c}{\textbf{Controls}} \\
   791	$T_{\chi_{-4}}$ at $\gamma_1(\zeta) = 14.13$ & 0.033 & 0.7 & Clean null \\
   792	$T_{\chi_3}$ at $\gamma_1(\zeta) = 14.13$ & 0.046 & 0.9 & Clean null \\
   793	\bottomrule
   794	\end{tabular}
   795	\end{center}
   796	
   797	The controls confirm selectivity: $T_\chi$ phase-locks at zeros of
   798	$L(s,\chi)$, \emph{not} at zeta zeros.  The three $T$-functions
   799	$T_{\mathrm{plain}}$, $T_{\chi_{-4}}$, $T_{\chi_3}$ are nearly
   800	uncorrelated ($|r| < 0.15$), confirming they carry independent
   801	arithmetic information.
   802	
   803	%% FIGURE: Four-panel L-function phase-lock comparison.
   804	%% Top-left: T_plain at gamma_1(zeta) -- strong lock (R=0.85).
   805	%% Top-right: T_chi4 at gamma_1(L,chi4) -- clear lock (R=0.16/0.54).
   806	%% Bottom-left: T_chi3 at gamma_1(L,chi3) -- very strong lock (R=0.28/0.64).
   807	%% Bottom-right: T_chi4 at gamma_1(zeta) -- clean null (R=0.03).
   808	
   809	\subsection{Extension to higher moduli}
   810	
   811	Testing all primes $\le 50{,}000$ (5{,}133 primes) with characters
   812	mod~5, 7, and~8:
   813	
   814	\begin{center}
   815	\begin{tabular}{lcc}
   816	\toprule
   817	Character & $\max(\sigma)$ & Verdict \\
   818	\midrule
   819	$\chi_5$ (quadratic) at own zero & 10.3 & Strong \\
   820	$\chi_7$ (quadratic) at own zero & 12.8 & Strong \\
   821	$\chi_8$ (real primitive) at own zero & 12.4 & Strong \\
   822	\bottomrule
   823	\end{tabular}
   824	\end{center}
   825	
   826	\textbf{Every character tested shows phase-lock at its own
   827	$L$-function zero}, with signal strengths $10$--$13\times$ random
   828	expectation.  Higher-order characters (cubic mod~7, quartic mod~5)
   829	also phase-lock, confirming universality across character orders.
   830	
   831	\subsection{Non-primitive characters}
   832	
   833	For $\chi \bmod 12$ induced from $\chi_3$, the phase-lock occurs at
   834	zeros of $L(s, \chi_3)$ (the primitive character), with
   835	$\sigma = 20.1\times$ for all primes---\emph{stronger} than the
   836	primitive signal.  This is predicted by the factorization
   837	$L(s, \chi) = L(s, \chi^*) \prod_{p \mid q,\, p \nmid q^*}
   838	(1 - \chi^*(p)/p^s)$: the zeros of the induced $L$-function are
   839	exactly those of the primitive $L$-function, plus finitely many from
   840	the Euler product correction.
   841	
   842	\subsection{Conjugate symmetry}
   843	
   844	$T_{\bar\chi}(N) = \overline{T_\chi(N)}$ exactly, since $\mu$ is
   845	real-valued: $M_{\bar\chi}(x) = \overline{M_\chi(x)}$.  Thus
   846	conjugate characters carry the same information.
   847	
   848	
   849	
   850	%% ================================================================
   851	%% SECTION 8: THE ORDERING PRINCIPLE
   852	%% ================================================================

/bin/zsh -lc "nl -ba paper/main_revised.tex | sed -n '430,540p'" in /Users/saar/Desktop/Farey-Local
 succeeded in 0ms:
   430	\]
   431	where $T(N) = \sum_{m=2}^{N} M(\lfloor N/m \rfloor)/m$ and $N = p-1$.
   432	\end{theorem}
   433	
   434	\begin{proof}[Proof sketch]
   435	The regression slope $\alpha = \Cov(D,f)/\Var(f)$ involves the quantity
   436	$\sum f \cdot D(f)$, which by classical Farey asymptotics (Franel--Landau)
   437	equals $-nR(N)/2 + O(1)$, where
   438	$R(N) = \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6}\sum_{m=1}^{N} M(\lfloor N/m\rfloor)/m$.
   439	Since $\Var(f) = 1/12 + O(1/N)$, we get $\alpha = -6R(N) + O(1/N)$.
   440	Expanding: $6R(N) = 1 + M(N) + T(N)$, and for $M(N) = -2$:
   441	$\alpha = 1 - T(N) + O(1/N)$.
   442	\end{proof}
   443	
   444	The chain of identities is now complete:
   445	\[
   446	  \Delta W(p) < 0
   447	  \;\Longleftrightarrow\; B + C > 0
   448	  \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \alpha + \rho > -1
   449	  \;\Longleftrightarrow\; T(N) < 2 + \rho.
   450	\]
   451	Since $\rho \approx -3.9$ for large~$p$, the threshold is approximately
   452	$T(N) < -1.9$.
   453	
   454	
   455	
   456	%% ================================================================
   457	%% SECTION 4: COMPUTATIONAL SIGN THEOREM AND COUNTEREXAMPLE
   458	%% ================================================================
   459	\section{Computational Sign Theorem and the First Counterexample}
   460	\label{sec:sign}
   461	
   462	\subsection{Sign Theorem to $10^5$}
   463	
   464	\begin{theorem}[Computational]\label{thm:sign}
   465	$\Delta W(p) < 0$ for all $4{,}617$ primes $11 \le p \le 10^5$ with
   466	$M(p) \le -3$.
   467	\end{theorem}
   468	
   469	This was verified by exact rational arithmetic over all qualifying
   470	primes.  For each prime, the four-term decomposition was evaluated
   471	by enumerating $\F_{p-1}$ via the mediant algorithm and computing
   472	$A$, $B$, $C$, $D$ exactly, with internal consistency checks
   473	($\sum \delta = 0$ per denominator, $\sum f\delta = C'/2$).
   474	
   475	The mechanism: for $p \le 10^5$, the sum $T(N)$ is uniformly negative
   476	(reaching $-50$ at $N \approx 10^5$), so $\alpha \approx 1 - T(N) \approx 51$,
   477	which far exceeds $|\rho| \approx 3.9$.  The inequality
   478	$\alpha + \rho > -1$ holds with enormous margin.
   479	
   480	\subsection{The first counterexample: $p = 243{,}799$}
   481	
   482	\begin{proposition}[Computational]\label{prop:counterexample}
   483	The first prime $p$ with $M(p) \le -3$ and $\Delta W(p) > 0$ is
   484	$p = 243{,}799$.
   485	\end{proposition}
   486	
   487	At this prime, $N = 243{,}798$, $M(N) = -2$, and:
   488	
   489	\begin{center}
   490	\begin{tabular}{ll}
   491	\toprule
   492	Quantity & Value \\
   493	\midrule
   494	$T(N)$ & $+0.165$ \\
   495	$\alpha$ & $0.835$ \\
   496	$\rho$ & $-3.887$ \\
   497	$B'/C'$ & $-3.052$ \\
   498	$D/A$ & $0.981$ \\
   499	$n'^2 \Delta W$ & $+6.65 \times 10^9$ \\
   500	\bottomrule
   501	\end{tabular}
   502	\end{center}
   503	
   504	\textbf{Failure mechanism.}  $T(N)$ crosses zero for the first time on
   505	the $M(N) = -2$ subsequence, causing $\alpha$ to drop from its typical
   506	value of $\sim 12$ (at $p \sim 10^5$) to~$0.835$.  Since
   507	$\rho \approx -3.9$ is stable, $\alpha + \rho = -3.05 < -1$, and
   508	$\Delta W > 0$.
   509	
   510	\textbf{This counterexample is not an anomaly}---it is the onset of
   511	a systematic pattern.  Among the 922 primes $p \le 10^7$ with
   512	$M(p) = -3$, 247 (26.8\%) have $T(N) > 0$ and hence $\Delta W > 0$.
   513	The fraction increases with~$p$: from $0\%$ below $10^5$, to $10\%$
   514	in $[10^5, 10^6)$, to $46\%$ in $[10^6, 10^7)$.
   515	
   516	%% FIGURE: Running fraction of T(N) > 0 primes vs p, showing the
   517	%% monotone increase from 0 toward ~1/2.  Include the prediction
   518	%% from the Rubinstein-Sarnak framework (dashed curve) for comparison.
   519	
   520	\subsection{Rho stability}
   521	
   522	The residual $\rho$ stabilizes rapidly:
   523	
   524	\begin{center}
   525	\begin{tabular}{ll}
   526	\toprule
   527	Range of $p$ & $\rho$ range \\
   528	\midrule
   529	$[13, 179]$ & $[-3.2, -1.3]$ \\
   530	$[1000, 5000]$ & $[-3.85, -3.64]$ \\
   531	$[5000, 100{,}000]$ & $[-3.89, -3.83]$ \\
   532	\bottomrule
   533	\end{tabular}
   534	\end{center}
   535	
   536	For $p > 5000$, $\rho$ is effectively constant at $\approx -3.88$.
   537	The sign of $\Delta W$ is therefore determined by $\alpha$, hence
   538	by~$T(N)$.
   539

/bin/zsh -lc "nl -ba experiments/PERRON_INTEGRAL_T.md | sed -n '1,220p'" in /Users/saar/Desktop/Farey-Local
 succeeded in 0ms:
     1	# Perron Integral Representation of T(N) and Chebyshev Bias for Farey Discrepancy
     2	
     3	## Date: 2026-03-30
     4	## Status: DERIVED (conditional on GRH+LI; all computations verified with mpmath at 30-digit precision)
     5	## Classification: C2 (collaborative, publication grade)
     6	## Connects to: N1 (Sign Theorem), N2 (Mertens-Wobble), Rubinstein-Sarnak (1994)
     7	
     8	---
     9	
    10	## 0. Summary of Results
    11	
    12	We derive the explicit formula for T(N) = sum_{m=2}^{N} M(floor(N/m))/m via the
    13	Perron integral, compute residues at the double pole s = 0 and at all nontrivial
    14	zeta zeros, and obtain three quantitative results:
    15	
    16	1. **Phase prediction**: The M(N/2) mechanism predicts T > 0 peaks at gamma_1*log(N) = **5.208** (mod 2*pi). Observed: **5.28**. Discrepancy: **0.07 radians** (1.1%).
    17	
    18	2. **Chebyshev bias**: Under GRH+LI, the limiting log-density of {N : T(N) > 0} is exactly **1/2** (symmetric distribution). The finite-N bias toward T < 0 is O(log(N)/sqrt(N)).
    19	
    20	3. **Density prediction**: At N ~ 10^7, the Rubinstein-Sarnak framework predicts P(T > 0) = 0.47. Observed among M(p)=-3 primes in [1M, 10M): **0.462**. Excellent match.
    21	
    22	---
    23	
    24	## 1. The Perron Integral
    25	
    26	### 1.1. Setup
    27	
    28	Define F(N) = sum_{m=1}^{N} M(floor(N/m))/m. Then T(N) = F(N) - M(N).
    29	
    30	The Dirichlet series identity:
    31	  sum_{n=1}^{infty} [sum_{d|n} mu(d)/d * (1/(n/d))] / n^s = ...
    32	
    33	More directly: the hyperbolic sum sum_{km <= N} mu(k)/m has generating function
    34	
    35	  (1/2*pi*i) integral_{(c)} N^s * zeta(s+1) / (s * zeta(s)) ds     (c > 1)
    36	
    37	since:
    38	  - mu(n) has Dirichlet series 1/zeta(s)
    39	  - 1/n has Dirichlet series zeta(s+1) (shifted)
    40	  - The Perron formula integral_{(c)} x^s/s ds = 1_{x>1}
    41	
    42	### 1.2. The integrand
    43	
    44	F(s) = N^s * zeta(s+1) / (s * zeta(s))
    45	
    46	Poles:
    47	  - **s = 0**: double pole (from 1/s and the pole of zeta(s+1) at s = 0)
    48	  - **s = rho_k**: simple poles at nontrivial zeros of zeta (from 1/zeta(s))
    49	  - **s = -2n**: simple poles at trivial zeros of zeta (minor contributions)
    50	
    51	---
    52	
    53	## 2. Residue at s = 0 (Double Pole)
    54	
    55	### 2.1. Laurent expansions near s = 0
    56	
    57	| Function | Expansion |
    58	|----------|-----------|
    59	| N^s | 1 + s*log(N) + s^2*(log N)^2/2 + ... |
    60	| zeta(s+1) | 1/s + gamma + gamma_1*s + ... (gamma_k = Stieltjes constants) |
    61	| zeta(s) | -1/2 + zeta'(0)*s + ... where zeta'(0) = -log(2*pi)/2 |
    62	| 1/zeta(s) | -2 + c_1*s + ... where c_1 = 2*log(2*pi) = 3.6758 |
    63	
    64	### 2.2. Laurent expansion of F(s)
    65	
    66	F(s) = [1 + s*log(N) + ...] * [1/s + gamma + ...] * (1/s) * [-2 + c_1*s + ...]
    67	
    68	The product (1/zeta(s)) * zeta(s+1) * (1/s) has the expansion:
    69	
    70	  [-2 + c_1*s + ...] * [1/s^2 + gamma/s + gamma_1 + ...] * [1 + s*log(N) + ...]
    71	
    72	Collecting terms:
    73	  = -2/s^2 + [-2*gamma + c_1 + (-2)*log(N)] / s + O(1)
    74	
    75	### 2.3. The residue
    76	
    77	**Res_{s=0} F(s) = -2*(log(N) + gamma) + 2*log(2*pi)**
    78	
    79	Numerical values:
    80	  - gamma (Euler-Mascheroni) = 0.5772156649
    81	  - zeta(0) = -1/2
    82	  - zeta'(0) = -log(2*pi)/2 = -0.9189385332
    83	  - 1/zeta(0) = -2
    84	  - Coefficient of s in 1/zeta(s): 2*log(2*pi) = 3.6757541328
    85	
    86	So:
    87	  **Res_{s=0} = -2*log(N) + 2.521323**
    88	
    89	For specific values:
    90	  - N = 1000: Res = -11.294
    91	  - N = 243798: Res = -22.287
    92	  - N = 10^7: Res = -29.728
    93	
    94	---
    95	
    96	## 3. Residues at Zeta Zeros
    97	
    98	### 3.1. Formula
    99	
   100	At s = rho_k where zeta(rho_k) = 0 (assuming RH: rho_k = 1/2 + i*gamma_k):
   101	
   102	  **Res_{s=rho_k} F(s) = N^{rho_k} * zeta(rho_k + 1) / (rho_k * zeta'(rho_k))**
   103	
   104	Define c_k = zeta(rho_k + 1) / (rho_k * zeta'(rho_k)). The contribution from
   105	the pair (rho_k, conj(rho_k)) is 2*Re[c_k * N^{rho_k}].
   106	
   107	### 3.2. Computed coefficients (first 10 zeros)
   108	
   109	| k | gamma_k | |c_k| | arg(c_k) |
   110	|---|---------|-------|----------|
   111	| 1 | 14.1347 | 0.04853 | -1.6016 |
   112	| 2 | 21.0220 | 0.02778 | -1.4873 |
   113	| 3 | 25.0109 | 0.02170 | -1.6683 |
   114	| 4 | 30.4249 | 0.01704 | -1.3369 |
   115	| 5 | 32.9351 | 0.01526 | -1.7992 |
   116	| 6 | 37.5862 | 0.01229 | -1.4577 |
   117	| 7 | 40.9187 | 0.01143 | -1.4516 |
   118	| 8 | 43.3271 | 0.01037 | -1.8304 |
   119	| 9 | 48.0052 | 0.00951 | -1.1557 |
   120	| 10 | 49.7738 | 0.00918 | -1.8396 |
   121	
   122	The dominant coefficient: **|c_1| = 0.04853, arg(c_1) = -1.6016 rad**.
   123	
   124	### 3.3. Amplitude ratios
   125	
   126	|c_1|/|c_2| = 1.75, so the first zero dominates but not overwhelmingly.
   127	The sum of all |c_k|^2 for k = 1..20 gives sigma = 0.101, with the first
   128	zero contributing 48% of the variance.
   129	
   130	---
   131	
   132	## 4. Explicit Formula for T(N)
   133	
   134	### 4.1. Full formula
   135	
   136	Under GRH:
   137	
   138	  **T(N) = [-2*log(N) + 2 - 2*gamma + 2*log(2*pi)] + sum_{rho} 2*Re[c_rho * N^rho] - M(N) + O(1)**
   139	
   140	For M(N) = -2:
   141	
   142	  **T(N) = -2*log(N) + 6.5213 + 0.0971 * sqrt(N) * cos(14.1347*log(N) - 1.6016) + [higher zeros] + O(1)**
   143	
   144	### 4.2. Behavior
   145	
   146	- **Drift term**: -2*log(N) + 6.52, which is negative for N > 26 and grows increasingly negative.
   147	- **Oscillatory terms**: amplitude ~ 0.097*sqrt(N), which grows much faster than |log(N)|.
   148	- **Crossover**: for N ~ 200K, the oscillatory amplitude becomes comparable to the drift, explaining why the first T > 0 counterexample appears at N = 243,798.
   149	
   150	### 4.3. T > 0 condition
   151	
   152	T > 0 when the oscillatory sum exceeds the drift:
   153	
   154	  sum_rho 2*Re[c_rho * N^rho] > 2*log(N) - 6.52
   155	
   156	For large N, the left side is O(sqrt(N)) while the right is O(log(N)), so T > 0
   157	occurs for roughly half of all N (in a logarithmic density sense).
   158	
   159	---
   160	
   161	## 5. Phase Window Analysis
   162	
   163	### 5.1. Direct Perron prediction
   164	
   165	The Perron coefficient c_1 predicts T > 0 peaks at:
   166	  gamma_1*log(N) = -arg(c_1) mod 2*pi = 1.60 (mod 2*pi)
   167	
   168	This is the phase where the FULL oscillatory term in T(N) is maximal.
   169	
   170	### 5.2. The M(N/2) decomposition
   171	
   172	T(N) is dominated by M(N/2)/2 (empirical correlation r = 0.95). The Mertens
   173	function explicit formula gives:
   174	
   175	  M(x) ~ 2*Re[d_1 * x^{rho_1}] where d_1 = 1/(rho_1 * zeta'(rho_1))
   176	
   177	Computed: |d_1| = 0.08914, arg(d_1) = -1.6933
   178	
   179	M(N/2) > 0 when:
   180	  gamma_1*log(N/2) + arg(d_1) is in (-pi/2, pi/2)
   181	  i.e., gamma_1*log(N) near gamma_1*log(2) - arg(d_1)
   182	  = 3.5143 - (-1.6933) = **5.2076** (mod 2*pi)
   183	
   184	### 5.3. Comparison with observations
   185	
   186	| Quantity | Value |
   187	|----------|-------|
   188	| Predicted peak (M(N/2) mechanism) | **5.208** |
   189	| Observed peak (circular mean of T > 0) | **5.28** |
   190	| Discrepancy | **0.072 rad (1.1%)** |
   191	| Phase shift gamma_1*log(2) mod 2*pi | 3.514 |
   192	| Direct Perron prediction | 1.602 |
   193	
   194	**The M(N/2) mechanism prediction matches the observed phase to 0.07 radians.**
   195	
   196	The direct Perron peak at 1.60 and the M(N/2) peak at 5.21 differ by 3.61, which
   197	is close to gamma_1*log(2) mod 2*pi = 3.51. This is not a coincidence -- the full
   198	Perron coefficient c_1 includes contributions from ALL m, while the M(N/2)/2 term
   199	isolates the m = 2 contribution. The m = 1 term (which contributes at phase 1.60)
   200	partially cancels in T(N) because it appears as -M(N), while the m = 2 term
   201	(phase-shifted by 3.51) dominates the remainder.
   202	
   203	### 5.4. Reconciliation of the two phase predictions
   204	
   205	The direct Perron formula gives the peak of T(N) + M(N), not of T(N) itself.
   206	Since T(N) = [sum from m=1..N] - M(N), and the m = 1 term IS M(N):
   207	
   208	  T(N) = sum_{m=2}^N M(floor(N/m))/m = [Perron sum] - M(N)
   209	
   210	The "M(N)" subtraction removes the m = 1 contribution at phase arg(d_1) = -1.69,
   211	leaving the dominant oscillation from m = 2 at phase arg(d_1) + gamma_1*log(2) = -1.69 + 3.51 = 1.82...
   212	
   213	Wait -- more carefully: the m = 2 contribution to T(N) has oscillatory phase
   214	arg(d_1) - gamma_1*log(2) = -1.69 - 3.51 = -5.21 = 1.07 mod 2*pi for T, but
   215	the POSITIVE peak of M(N/2) maps to gamma_1*log(N) = 5.21 as computed.
   216	
   217	The M(N/2) analysis correctly captures the empirically dominant channel, while
   218	the Perron formula gives the full analytic structure including all m.
   219	
   220	---

experiments/DISCOVERIES_AND_RH.md:14:| Sign Theorem: DeltaW(p) < 0 for M(p) <= -3 | Proved analytically for large p + computed for p <= 100K | Primes with sufficiently negative Mertens decrease Farey discrepancy |
experiments/DISCOVERIES_AND_RH.md:37:We have W(N) = W(1) + sum_{k=2}^{N} DeltaW(k). Our Sign Theorem says DeltaW(p) < 0 when M(p) <= -3, meaning those primes INCREASE W (our convention: DeltaW = W(N-1) - W(N), so DeltaW < 0 means W goes up).
experiments/DISCOVERIES_AND_RH.md:41:1. The Sign Theorem only covers primes with M(p) <= -3. Primes with M(p) > -3 (including the counterexample at p = 92,173 where DeltaW > 0) go the other direction. The BALANCE between positive-M and negative-M primes determines the cumulative behavior.
experiments/DISCOVERIES_AND_RH.md:213:### F1. An Unconditional Sign Theorem for ALL Primes (Not Just M(p) <= -3)
experiments/DISCOVERIES_AND_RH.md:217:**Why it matters:** The counterexample at p = 92,173 (M(p) > 0, DeltaW > 0) shows this is FALSE as stated. But a weaker version -- DeltaW(p) < 0 for "most" primes in a density sense -- combined with magnitude bounds could give:
experiments/DISCOVERIES_AND_RH.md:243:**What:** Find a quantity Q(N) that is STRICTLY MONOTONE at every step (not just at primes with M(p) <= -3) and whose rate of growth/decay encodes RH.
experiments/DISCOVERIES_AND_RH.md:264:**Gap:** The cancellation between prime damage and composite healing is precisely the arithmetic that encodes zeta zeros. We know 96% of composites heal, and our Sign Theorem covers M(p) <= -3 primes. But the positive-M primes and the MAGNITUDES are uncontrolled.
experiments/DISCOVERIES_AND_RH.md:292:1. **The Sign Theorem does not constrain W(N) growth.** Sign information without magnitude bounds does not help with the cumulative sum.
experiments/DISCOVERIES_AND_RH.md:302:Our framework provides a NEW DECOMPOSITION of a KNOWN RH-equivalent quantity (the Franel L2 sum) into per-step contributions controlled by the Mertens function. This decomposition is mathematically valid, computationally revealing, and produces genuine theorems (Sign Theorem, Bypass Theorem, N11 identity). But it does not provide a new ROUTE to RH because:
experiments/DISCOVERIES_AND_RH.md:313:- **Publishable theorems** (Sign Theorem for M(p) <= -3, Bypass Theorem, permutation identity N11)
experiments/DISCOVERIES_AND_RH.md:324:"The per-step Farey discrepancy DeltaW(p), which we prove is negative for all primes with M(p) <= -3, provides a new dynamical decomposition of the Franel-Landau L2 sum into arithmetically interpretable components, but does not yield unconditional bounds beyond those obtainable from the prime number theorem."

experiments/COMPOSITES_INDEPENDENCE_CLOSE.md:73:## What About Approach 4 (Direct Bypass via Sign Theorem)?
experiments/COMPOSITES_INDEPENDENCE_CLOSE.md:75:The original approach 4 suggested: For N = 2p with M(2p) <= -3, use M(2p) = M(2p-1) + 1 to get M(2p-1) <= -4, then invoke the Sign Theorem on the prime p.
experiments/COMPOSITES_INDEPENDENCE_CLOSE.md:81:The Sign Theorem says: for primes p with M(p) <= -3, we have DeltaW(p) < 0 (the prime p heals). But the question here is about the COMPOSITE 2p, not the prime p. The Sign Theorem for primes and the healing criterion for composites are related but distinct:
experiments/COMPOSITES_INDEPENDENCE_CLOSE.md:83:- **Prime p:** DeltaW(p) < 0 when M(p) <= -3 (Sign Theorem)
experiments/COMPOSITES_INDEPENDENCE_CLOSE.md:86:So approach 4 would say: if M(2p) <= -3, then M(2p-1) <= -4 < -1, so 2p heals. This is correct but trivial from the Mertens-healing criterion itself -- it just says "deeply negative M implies healing." It does NOT need the Sign Theorem for primes at all.
experiments/COMPOSITES_INDEPENDENCE_CLOSE.md:88:**Conclusion on approach 4:** It works but is unnecessary. The observation M(2p) <= -3 => healing is immediate from M(2p-1) <= -4 < 0 and the Mertens criterion. The Sign Theorem for primes is not invoked.
experiments/COMPOSITES_INDEPENDENCE_CLOSE.md:143:**Key insight:** The proof requires only the Prime Number Theorem. No independence of mu and M is needed, no RH assumption is invoked, and the Sign Theorem for primes is not used. The argument is that non-healing composites are overwhelmingly concentrated among numbers with few prime factors, and such numbers have density zero.
experiments/COMPOSITES_INDEPENDENCE_CLOSE.md:154:| 4 | Bypass via Sign Theorem | NO (and has sign error: mu(2p)=+1 not -1) | Observation is correct but trivial from Mertens criterion |

experiments/PROOF_ADVERSARIAL_AUDIT.md:1:# ADVERSARIAL AUDIT: Sign Theorem Proof
experiments/PROOF_ADVERSARIAL_AUDIT.md:10:The claimed proof of "For every prime p >= 11 with M(p) <= -3: DeltaW(p) < 0 (unconditionally)" contains **at least 5 fatal flaws**, **3 serious gaps**, and **several overstatements**. The proof as stated is **NOT valid**. What exists is a plausible heuristic argument supported by extensive computation, not a rigorous proof.
experiments/PROOF_ADVERSARIAL_AUDIT.md:20:The claim states: "Bypass: B >= 0 for M(p) <= -3 (verified for 210 primes to p ~ 3000), so suffices C+D > A+1."
experiments/PROOF_ADVERSARIAL_AUDIT.md:176:## SERIOUS GAP 3: Restriction to M(p) <= -3
experiments/PROOF_ADVERSARIAL_AUDIT.md:180:The theorem is stated for primes with M(p) <= -3 only. This is a significant restriction. The proof files show that:
experiments/PROOF_ADVERSARIAL_AUDIT.md:183:- The M(p) <= -3 condition is used to ensure B >= 0 empirically (from the "B >= 0 for M(p) <= -3" claim verified for 210 primes)
experiments/PROOF_ADVERSARIAL_AUDIT.md:185:But this raises the question: what happens for primes with M(p) > -3? Are there primes where DeltaW > 0? The computational data shows DeltaW <= 0 for ALL primes up to 100,000, not just those with M(p) <= -3. So why restrict?
experiments/PROOF_ADVERSARIAL_AUDIT.md:187:The restriction appears to be an artifact of the proof strategy (needing B >= 0 which is easier to verify/believe when M(p) is sufficiently negative). This suggests the theorem as stated is weaker than the data supports, and the M(p) <= -3 condition is a crutch for the unproved B >= 0 step.
experiments/PROOF_ADVERSARIAL_AUDIT.md:209:The claim says "verified for all 4,617 M(p) <= -3 primes to p = 100,000." But the proof strategy requires the analytical argument to take over before the computational base ends. The analytical crossover at P_0 ~ 65,500 is computed using unproved constants. If the true P_0 is larger than 100,000, there is a gap.
experiments/PROOF_ADVERSARIAL_AUDIT.md:230:   - B >= 0 for all primes with M(p) <= -3 up to p ~ 200,000
experiments/PROOF_ADVERSARIAL_AUDIT.md:234:   - B >= 0 for all primes (or even for all M(p) <= -3 primes)
experiments/PROOF_ADVERSARIAL_AUDIT.md:238:   - The Sign Theorem itself for p > 100,000

experiments/RH_BRIDGE_ANALYSIS.md:73:### What the Sign Theorem says about RH
experiments/RH_BRIDGE_ANALYSIS.md:75:Our empirical result: DeltaW(p) < 0 for primes with M(p) <= -3 (verified to p = 100,000 with one exception at p = 92,173 where M = -2).
experiments/RH_BRIDGE_ANALYSIS.md:79:For RH: Under RH, M(N) = O(N^{1/2+epsilon}), which means M(p) oscillates around zero with moderate amplitude. Our Sign Theorem says that at primes where M(p) < -2, the Farey discrepancy decreases. Under RH, this happens roughly half the time. The OTHER half (M(p) > 0), where DeltaW > 0 (discrepancy increases), is where the Farey sequence temporarily "de-equidistributes."
experiments/RH_BRIDGE_ANALYSIS.md:85:Our result that B + C > 0 for all primes p >= 11 (with M(p) <= -3) means:
experiments/RH_BRIDGE_ANALYSIS.md:91:Our data shows D/A -> 1 as p -> infinity. The Sign Theorem thus becomes a statement about the **residual** A - D, which is small and sign-determined by M(p). This near-cancellation D/A ~ 1 is itself interesting -- it says the new fractions contribute discrepancy that almost exactly cancels the dilution benefit. The Mertens function tips the balance.
experiments/RH_BRIDGE_ANALYSIS.md:101:3. **Our analytical proofs are conditional on M(p) <= -3.** The unconditional sign theorem for all primes remains open. The exceptional prime p = 92,173 with M(p) = -2 shows the threshold matters.
experiments/RH_BRIDGE_ANALYSIS.md:229:3. **The conditional proof (M(p) <= -3).** Conditioning on the value of M(p) is circular from the RH perspective -- RH determines the statistics of M(p). Proving DeltaW < 0 when M(p) <= -3 is not RH progress; it is a statement about a specific regime.
experiments/RH_BRIDGE_ANALYSIS.md:252:- Partial analytical proofs (B+C > 0 for M(p) <= -3 primes, Rearrangement Lemma)
experiments/RH_BRIDGE_ANALYSIS.md:258:- An unconditional proof of the Sign Theorem for all primes
experiments/RH_BRIDGE_ANALYSIS.md:281:2. **Pursue the unconditional Sign Theorem** as the main open problem. If achievable, this is a genuine new theorem about Farey sequences.

experiments/B_TAIL_CLOSE.md:3:## Status: CLOSED -- B' > 0 proved for all M(p) <= -3 primes

experiments/CREATIVE_BYPASS_IDEAS.md:1:# Creative Bypass Ideas: Proving the Sign Theorem Without Sigma E^2 >= c*p^2*log(p)
experiments/CREATIVE_BYPASS_IDEAS.md:10:**Sign Theorem (target):** For every prime p >= 11, Delta W(p) < 0.
experiments/CREATIVE_BYPASS_IDEAS.md:12:**Current approach:** Four-term decomposition Delta W = A' - B' - C' + 1 - D'. For M(p) <= -3 primes, B' >= 0, so we need C' + D' > A' + 1. This reduces to showing D' ~ A' (near-cancellation) and C' = Sigma delta^2 is large enough to absorb the deficit. The sticking point is making the C' lower bound rigorous for ALL primes, including those with large positive M(p) where B' + C' can be negative (R(p) < -1/2 observed at p=1399, 1409, 1423).
experiments/CREATIVE_BYPASS_IDEAS.md:14:**Key obstacle:** The decomposition approach splits naturally into M(p) <= -3 (where it nearly works) and M(p) > -3 (where B+C can be negative, requiring a fundamentally different argument). A unified proof would be more satisfying.
experiments/CREATIVE_BYPASS_IDEAS.md:121:Instead of proving Delta W(p) < 0 only at primes, prove W(N+1) < W(N) for ALL N >= some threshold. Then the Sign Theorem for primes follows as a special case.
experiments/CREATIVE_BYPASS_IDEAS.md:140:- If proved for all N, the Sign Theorem follows trivially
experiments/CREATIVE_BYPASS_IDEAS.md:284:**Verdict:** This is THE cleanest formulation of the problem, and worth writing up as the framework for a proof. But it does NOT bypass the cross-term bound. The insight is: the Sign Theorem is equivalent to showing that the L2 inner product between the Farey discrepancy function and the p-grid discrepancy function is not too negative. This is a nice reformulation but not a bypass.
experiments/CREATIVE_BYPASS_IDEAS.md:437:1. For M(p) <= -3: use the bypass (C' > deficit + 1, nearly proved in UNCONDITIONAL_PROOF_SKETCH.md)
experiments/CREATIVE_BYPASS_IDEAS.md:449:4. **Formalize Path C two-class proof** -- nearly complete for M(p) <= -3, needs the M(p) > -3 case
experiments/CREATIVE_BYPASS_IDEAS.md:455:Most "creative bypass" ideas reduce to the SAME cross-term bounding problem, just reformulated. The genuine new insight is the **variational/L2 CDF formulation** (Idea 6 / Path A), which shows that the Sign Theorem is equivalent to a Cauchy-Schwarz-type bound in L2 function space. This might be tractable because the p-grid discrepancy function g has very small L2 norm (~1/p), while the Farey discrepancy function h has larger norm (~sqrt(log(p)/p^3)), and their inner product is bounded by the product of norms. The question is whether this Cauchy-Schwarz bound, combined with the explicit dilution factor (n/n')^2 < 1, is TIGHT ENOUGH to close the proof.
experiments/CREATIVE_BYPASS_IDEAS.md:457:The second genuinely new direction is **Path C (two-class proof)**: accept that M(p) <= -3 and M(p) > -3 require different arguments, and close each separately. This is less elegant but potentially achievable.

experiments/MAYER_SPECTRAL_PROOF.md:1:# Mayer Transfer Operator and Spectral Proof of the Sign Theorem
experiments/MAYER_SPECTRAL_PROOF.md:13:This document investigates whether the Mayer transfer operator framework can provide a spectral proof of our Sign Theorem (that Delta_W(p) < 0 for all primes p >= 11). The short answer: **there is no clean path from Mayer's framework to our Sign Theorem, but there are suggestive structural parallels that deserve further study.** The honest assessment is that the existing literature connects Farey sequences to spectral theory primarily through equidistribution and the Riemann Hypothesis, not through the kind of prime-by-prime monotonicity statement our Sign Theorem requires.
experiments/MAYER_SPECTRAL_PROOF.md:183:## 6. Can the Sign Theorem Follow from Spectral Monotonicity?
experiments/MAYER_SPECTRAL_PROOF.md:195:**The core difficulty:** The Mayer/horocycle framework gives *asymptotic* information about the distribution of Farey fractions (equidistribution rates as N -> infinity). Our Sign Theorem is a *pointwise* statement about what happens at each individual prime p.
experiments/MAYER_SPECTRAL_PROOF.md:275:### 8.3 But Not the Sign Theorem
experiments/MAYER_SPECTRAL_PROOF.md:331:1. **A clean spectral monotonicity argument for the Sign Theorem.** The Hecke eigenvalues oscillate (Sato-Tate), so there is no "monotone increase of the leading eigenvalue through primes."
experiments/MAYER_SPECTRAL_PROOF.md:335:3. **The pair correlation / BCZ results implying the Sign Theorem.** These are asymptotic results about the limiting distribution, not about individual prime steps.
experiments/MAYER_SPECTRAL_PROOF.md:337:### 10.4 The Gap Between Franel-Landau and the Sign Theorem
experiments/MAYER_SPECTRAL_PROOF.md:341:The Sign Theorem says: W_N increases at EVERY prime step (not just on average).
experiments/MAYER_SPECTRAL_PROOF.md:345:**Assessment:** A spectral proof of the Sign Theorem would likely require understanding cancellations among zeta zero contributions at individual primes, which is a problem of comparable difficulty to understanding short-interval prime counts. This is not a tractable path with current technology.
experiments/MAYER_SPECTRAL_PROOF.md:419:However, **none of these connections directly yield a proof of the Sign Theorem.** The fundamental problem is that spectral methods give asymptotic/average information, while the Sign Theorem is a pointwise statement about individual primes.
experiments/MAYER_SPECTRAL_PROOF.md:423:1. **DO NOT pursue** a direct spectral proof of the Sign Theorem via Mayer operators. The gap is too large.
experiments/MAYER_SPECTRAL_PROOF.md:431:5. **The most honest path to the Sign Theorem remains our arithmetic/algebraic approach** (the four-term decomposition, the B+C geometric identity, the M(p) correlation). The spectral framework is beautiful context but not a proof tool for this specific result.

experiments/ADVERSARIAL_CORRECTION_NEGATIVITY.md:95:Section 4.1, Case 3: "For all 81 remaining M(p) = -3 primes in (179, 20000], the streaming computation confirms correction/C' < 0 < 1/2."
experiments/ADVERSARIAL_CORRECTION_NEGATIVITY.md:142:Could there exist a very large prime p with M(p) = -3 where T(N) > 0? The M(p) = -3 condition constrains M(p-1) = -2, but it does NOT prevent wild oscillations in the sum T(N) = sum_{m=2}^{N} M(floor(N/m))/m. The Mertens function M(x) oscillates wildly (unconditionally, |M(x)| grows at least as fast as x^{1/2} infinitely often). For a sufficiently pathological N, the partial sums in T(N) could in principle conspire to be positive.
experiments/ADVERSARIAL_CORRECTION_NEGATIVITY.md:165:However, there is a subtler issue: the M(p) <= -3 condition is used in DECORRELATION_PROOF.md Section 4.7 ("Making it Unconditional for M(p) <= -3") where it states that "The M(p) <= -3 condition means mu(k) = -1 for at least 3 more primes k <= p than mu(k) = +1." This characterization of M(p) <= -3 is incorrect -- the Mertens function counts ALL mu(k) for k <= p, not just primes. The constraint M(p) = -3 involves the cumulative sum of mu over all integers, including composite numbers with even or odd numbers of prime factors.
experiments/ADVERSARIAL_CORRECTION_NEGATIVITY.md:167:### Severity: MODERATE. No circularity, but the auxiliary claim about what M(p) <= -3 "means" is wrong (though not load-bearing for the argument).

experiments/large_scale_findings.md:79:**MAJOR FINDING**: The tightest M(p)/sqrt(p) approaches 0 as approximately -C/sqrt(p). Fitting: at p ~ X, tightest ~ -3/sqrt(X). This is consistent with M(p) = -3 being achievable at arbitrarily large primes (since M(p) = -3 gives M/sqrt(p) = -3/sqrt(p) -> 0). No sign of the sequence "running out" of M=-3 primes.

experiments/TWIN_PRIMES_ASSESSMENT.md:8:We investigated whether our Farey per-step discrepancy discoveries (Sign Theorem, bridge identity, four-term decomposition) have implications for the twin primes conjecture. After computational analysis of 1,222 twin prime pairs up to 100K and analytical examination of the bridge identity, the answer is **clearly negative**. The correlations we observe between twin primes are entirely explained by proximity effects that apply to ALL close prime pairs, with nothing twin-specific.

experiments/RHO_ANALYTICAL_BOUND.md:299:primes cluster in this exceptional set. In fact, the M(p) = -3 condition makes the permutations

experiments/CLEAN_PROOF_WRITEUP.md:258:**(b)** The phase concentration follows from the dominance of the first zero. Writing $c_1 N^{\rho_1} = |c_1|\sqrt{N}\,e^{i(\gamma_1 \log N + \arg c_1)}$, the real part is maximized when $\gamma_1 \log N + \arg c_1 \equiv 0 \pmod{2\pi}$. Since $T(N)$ is dominated by the $M(N/2)/2$ channel (empirically $r = 0.95$), and $M(N/2)$ oscillates with phase $\gamma_1 \log(N/2)$, the $T > 0$ events concentrate at phase $\gamma_1 \log 2 - \arg(d_1) \pmod{2\pi}$.
experiments/CLEAN_PROOF_WRITEUP.md:260:Numerically: $\arg(d_1) = -1.693$, $\gamma_1 \log 2 = 9.80 \equiv 3.514 \pmod{2\pi}$, giving a predicted peak at $3.514 + 1.693 = 5.208$.

experiments/CLEAN_PROOF_WRITEUP.md:307:| Fraction with $T > 0$ in $[10^6, 10^7)$ | 0.462 | 0.47 | 0.008 |
experiments/CLEAN_PROOF_WRITEUP.md:325:The 247 primes with $T(N) > 0$ (among 922 $M(p) = -3$ primes up to $10^7$) form only 16 runs, with run lengths up to 81 consecutive primes. The longest $T < 0$ run has 254 consecutive primes. This extreme clustering is explained by the slow $\gamma_1$-oscillation: when $\gamma_1 \log N$ is in the favorable phase window, all nearby primes share $T > 0$.

experiments/PHYSICS_CONNECTION_ANALYSIS.md:10:Our discovery that the per-step Farey discrepancy increases at primes (the Sign Theorem: dW(p) < 0 for all primes p >= 11) sits at the intersection of several deep mathematical physics structures. After thorough investigation, the connections range from genuinely rigorous (Franel-Landau, Mayer transfer operators) to deeply suggestive but unproven (trace formula analogies) to purely speculative (zero mode interpretation). This document is honest about which is which.
experiments/PHYSICS_CONNECTION_ANALYSIS.md:77:The fact that B + C > 0 (for M(p) <= -3, and likely always) means: inserting the new Farey fractions with denominator p INCREASES the squared discrepancy. In spectral terms: adding a prime's worth of new eigenvalues increases disorder, despite the new points partially filling gaps. This is a concrete, quantitative statement about how primes perturb the Farey spectrum.

experiments/CA_RATIO_PROOF.md:10:**Remark.** For the Sign Theorem, the unconditional bound (1) suffices: since 1 - D/A = O(exp(-c*sqrt(log p))) and 1/log^2(p) >> exp(-c*sqrt(log p)) for all p >= 7, the condition C/A > 1 - D/A holds for all sufficiently large p.      1	# Adversarial Audit: Perron Integral Derivation for T(N) -- SECOND PASS
     2	
     3	## Date: 2026-03-31
     4	## Auditor: Hostile Referee (independent agent, adversarial frame)
     5	## Source document: PERRON_INTEGRAL_T.md (2026-03-30)
     6	## Previous audit: ADVERSARIAL_PERRON.md (2026-03-30) -- missed the +2 constant error
     7	## Verdict: ONE ARITHMETIC ERROR FOUND (constant term +2.0 overcounting). Core claims SURVIVE.
     8	
     9	---
    10	
    11	## Executive Summary
    12	
    13	The Perron integral derivation is **mostly correct** but contains one concrete arithmetic error in the explicit formula constant (Section 4.1), plus several presentation issues. The phase prediction (5.208 vs observed 5.28) and the density-1/2 claim are unaffected by the error. The Rubinstein-Sarnak P(T>0) predictions are slightly affected but remain qualitatively valid.
    14	
    15	**The previous audit (2026-03-30) missed this error.** That audit rated the document "no fatal flaws found." This second pass found a clear +2.0 arithmetic overcounting, confirmed by brute-force computation.
    16	
    17	### Scorecard
    18	
    19	| Claim | Verdict | Details |
    20	|-------|---------|---------|
    21	| Perron integral representation | CORRECT | Dirichlet series identity verified analytically and by brute force |
    22	| Double pole residue at s=0 | CORRECT (formula) | Res = -2(logN + gamma) + 2log(2pi). Verified by contour integration to 6+ digits |
    23	| Numerical residue values (Sec 2.3) | MINOR ERROR | N=10^7: document says -29.728, correct value is -29.715 (0.04% off, rounding) |
    24	| Zeta zero coefficients c_k (Sec 3.2) | CORRECT | All 10 values verified independently to 5 significant figures |
    25	| c_1 value | CORRECT | -0.00149 - 0.04851i, |c_1| = 0.04853, arg(c_1) = -1.6016 |
    26	| d_1 value | CORRECT | -0.01089 - 0.08847i, |d_1| = 0.08914, arg(d_1) = -1.6933 |
    27	| Phase prediction 5.208 | CORRECT | gamma_1 * log(2) - arg(d_1) = 5.2076, matches to 4 figures |
    28	| **Explicit formula constant (Sec 4.1)** | **ERROR (+2.0)** | Document claims 6.52 for M(N)=-2; correct value is 4.52 |
    29	| Oscillatory amplitude 2|c_1| | CORRECT | 0.09706, document rounds to 0.0971 |
    30	| sigma from 20 zeros | CORRECT | sigma^2 = 0.01018, sigma = 0.1009 |
    31	| Density 1/2 claim | CORRECT (conditional) | Follows from R-S framework under GRH+LI; argument is sound |
    32	| P(T>0) at N=10^7 | APPROXIMATELY CORRECT | Gaussian approx gives 0.46-0.47 regardless of which constant; matches observation |
    33	
    34	---
    35	
    36	## Detailed Findings
    37	
    38	### 1. Perron Integral Representation (Section 1) -- CORRECT
    39	
    40	The Dirichlet series identity is standard and correct:
    41	
    42	- mu(n) has Dirichlet series 1/zeta(s)
    43	- The arithmetic function 1/n has Dirichlet series zeta(s+1)
    44	- The Dirichlet convolution h(n) = sum_{d|n} mu(d)/(n/d) has series zeta(s+1)/zeta(s)
    45	- Perron's formula gives sum_{n<=N} h(n) = (1/2pi i) int N^s zeta(s+1)/(s zeta(s)) ds
    46	
    47	Verified by brute force: the hyperbolic sum sum_{km<=N} mu(k)/m equals sum_{m=1}^N M(floor(N/m))/m (tested at N=1000, exact match to 10 digits).
    48	
    49	**No issues found.**
    50	
    51	### 2. Double Pole Residue (Section 2) -- CORRECT
    52	
    53	The Laurent expansion verified step by step:
    54	
    55	- zeta(s+1) = 1/s + gamma + gamma_1 s + ... : standard
    56	- zeta(s) = -1/2 + zeta'(0) s + ... where zeta'(0) = -log(2pi)/2 : verified numerically
    57	- 1/zeta(s) = -2 + 2 log(2pi) s + ... : verified by computing f'(0) = -zeta'(0)/zeta(0)^2 = 2 log(2pi) = 3.6758. Also verified by numerical difference quotient at h = 10^{-15}.
    58	
    59	The residue at s = 0:
    60	
    61	    Res_{s=0} F(s) = -2(log N + gamma) + 2 log(2pi) = -2 log N + 2.5213
    62	
    63	Verified by numerical contour integration (circular contour, radius 10^{-4}) at N = 1000, 243798, 10^7. All match to 4+ decimal places.
    64	
    65	The document's specific value at N = 10^7 has a minor rounding discrepancy (claims -29.728, correct is -29.715), but this is inconsequential.
    66	
    67	**No substantive issues.**
    68	
    69	### 3. ARITHMETIC ERROR: Constant Term in Section 4.1
    70	
    71	**This is the main finding of the audit.**
    72	
    73	Section 4.1 states:
    74	
    75	    T(N) = [-2 log(N) + 2 - 2 gamma + 2 log(2 pi)] + sum_rho 2 Re[c_rho N^rho] - M(N) + O(1)
    76	
    77	And for M(N) = -2:
    78	
    79	    T(N) = -2 log(N) + 6.5213 + 0.0971 sqrt(N) cos(14.1347 log(N) - 1.6016) + ...
    80	
    81	**The correct formula is:**
    82	
    83	    T(N) = [-2 log(N) - 2 gamma + 2 log(2 pi)] + sum_rho 2 Re[c_rho N^rho] - M(N) + O(1)
    84	
    85	Note: there is NO "+2" inside the brackets. The Perron residue at s = 0 is exactly -2 log(N) - 2 gamma + 2 log(2pi), with NO additional constant.
    86	
    87	**Proof:** The Laurent expansion of F(s) = N^s zeta(s+1)/(s zeta(s)) near s = 0:
    88	
    89	    F(s) = (1/s)(1 + s log N + ...)(1/s + gamma + ...)(- 2 + 2 log(2pi) s + ...)
    90	    = (-2/s^2) + (-2 log N - 2 gamma + 2 log(2pi))/s + O(1)
    91	
    92	The coefficient of 1/s (i.e., the residue) is -2 log N - 2 gamma + 2 log(2pi). There is no "+2" term.
    93	
    94	For M(N) = -2, the correct constant is:
    95	
    96	    -2 gamma + 2 log(2pi) + 2 = -1.1544 + 3.6758 + 2 = **4.5214**
    97	
    98	NOT 6.5213. The document overcounts by exactly +2.0.
    99	
   100	**Confirmed by brute force:** At N = 1000, the Perron formula (residue + 50 zeta zeros) gives F(1000) = -9.088, compared to the exact value F(1000) = -9.193. With M(1000) = 2, T(1000) = -11.193 actual vs -11.088 predicted. The predictions are consistent with a constant of 4.52, NOT 6.52.
   101	
   102	**Root cause:** The author most likely computed -M(N) = +2 and also erroneously added a "+2" to the Perron residue, double-counting one factor of 2.
   103	
   104	**Impact assessment:**
   105	- Phase prediction: UNAFFECTED (depends only on oscillatory terms)
   106	- Density 1/2: UNAFFECTED (threshold delta(N) -> 0 regardless)
   107	- P(T > 0) finite-N: SLIGHTLY affected. The threshold delta(N) = (2 log N - C)/sqrt(N) changes by 2/sqrt(N), which at N = 10^7 is 6.3 x 10^{-4}, shifting P(T > 0) by approximately 0.003. Qualitative conclusions unchanged.
   108	
   109	**Recommended fix:** Remove the "+2" from Section 4.1 brackets. The general formula should be:
   110	
   111	    T(N) = Res_{s=0} + sum_rho Res_{rho} - M(N) + O(1)
   112	         = [-2 logN - 2 gamma + 2 log(2pi)] + [oscillatory] - M(N) + O(1)
   113	
   114	For M(N) = -2: T(N) = -2 logN + 4.52 + 0.0971 sqrt(N) cos(14.13 logN - 1.60) + ...
   115	
   116	### 4. Zeta Zero Coefficients (Section 3) -- CORRECT
   117	
   118	All 10 coefficients c_k = zeta(rho_k + 1)/(rho_k zeta'(rho_k)) verified independently using mpmath at 50-digit precision. All |c_k| and arg(c_k) match to the precision given in the table. The formula for the residue at a simple zero of zeta in the denominator is standard. **No issues.**
   119	
   120	### 5. Phase Prediction (Section 5) -- CORRECT
   121	
   122	- gamma_1 log(2) mod 2pi = 3.5143 : verified
   123	- arg(d_1) = -1.6933 : verified (d_1 = 1/(rho_1 zeta'(rho_1)))
   124	- Predicted phase = 3.5143 - (-1.6933) = 5.2076 : verified
   125	- Observed: 5.28, discrepancy 0.072 rad (1.1%)
   126	
   127	**Presentation issue:** Section 5.4 is stream-of-consciousness with a "Wait -- more carefully:" in the middle. The reconciliation between the direct Perron prediction (1.60) and the M(N/2) prediction (5.21) is physically reasonable but needs a cleaner write-up.
   128	
   129	### 6. Density 1/2 Claim (Section 6) -- CORRECT under GRH + LI
   130	
   131	The Rubinstein-Sarnak framework applies correctly:
   132	
   133	1. The explicit formula has the standard form: constant drift + sum of N^{rho} oscillations
   134	2. Under LI (Linear Independence of zeta zero ordinates), the phases gamma_k log N become equidistributed mod 2pi
   135	3. The random variable Y = sum 2|c_k| cos(theta_k + arg(c_k)) is symmetric about 0
   136	4. The threshold delta(N) = O(log N / sqrt(N)) vanishes
   137	5. Therefore P(Y > delta) -> P(Y > 0) = 1/2
   138	
   139	**Criticism:** The observed P(T > 0) = 0.462 is measured among M(p) = -3 primes, NOT among all integers. The R-S theory predicts log-density over ALL N. The restriction to M(p) = -3 primes introduces a selection bias. The document acknowledges this (Section 8.3) but the comparison table (Section 6.3) should carry a more prominent caveat.
   140	
   141	### 7. Rubinstein-Sarnak P(T > 0) Numerical Check
   142	
   143	Using the Gaussian approximation with sigma = 0.1009 from 20 zeros:
   144	
   145	| Constant C | delta(10^7) | P(T > 0) Gaussian |
   146	|-----------|-------------|-------------------|
   147	| 2.52 (Res only) | 0.00940 | 0.463 |
   148	| 4.52 (correct, M = -2) | 0.00876 | 0.465 |
   149	| 6.52 (document, erroneous) | 0.00813 | 0.468 |
   150	
   151	All values are in range 0.46-0.47. The +2 error shifts the prediction by only 0.003. **The match with observed 0.462 is robust despite the error.**
   152	
   153	### 8. Brute Force Cross-Check
   154	
   155	F(N) computed by direct summation vs Perron prediction (residue at s = 0 + 50 zeta zero pairs):
   156	
   157	| N | F(N) actual | F(N) Perron | Difference | T(N) actual | T(N) Perron |
   158	|---|------------|-------------|------------|------------|-------------|
   159	| 100 | -4.636 | -4.779 | 0.143 | -5.636 | -5.779 |
   160	| 500 | -12.334 | -11.944 | 0.390 | -6.334 | -5.944 |
   161	| 1000 | -9.193 | -9.088 | 0.106 | -11.193 | -11.088 |
   162	| 2000 | -7.596 | -7.831 | 0.235 | -12.596 | -12.831 |
   163	
   164	The Perron formula with 50 zeros predicts F(N) to within 0.1-0.4, with accuracy improving as N grows. Remaining error from higher zeros, trivial zeros, and truncation.
   165	
   166	---
   167	
   168	## Issues Summary
   169	
   170	### ERRORS (must fix)
   171	
   172	1. **Section 4.1 constant term: +2.0 overcounting.** The explicit formula has an erroneous "+2" in the brackets. Correct constant for M(N) = -2 is 4.52, not 6.52. Downstream impact is minor but the formula as written is wrong.
   173	
   174	2. **Section 2.3 minor rounding:** N = 10^7 residue stated as -29.728, correct value is -29.715.
   175	
   176	### WARNINGS (should address)
   177	
   178	3. **Section 4.1 mixes general and specific.** The general Perron formula and the M(N) = -2 specialization need clearer separation.
   179	
   180	4. **Section 5.4 is unfinished.** Contains "Wait -- more carefully:" followed by a confused re-derivation. Needs clean rewrite.
   181	
   182	5. **Section 6.3 selection bias.** R-S theory applies to all N; comparing with M(p) = -3 primes is theoretically unjustified without a conditional density argument.
   183	
   184	6. **Error terms never bounded.** Acknowledged in Section 8.3 but the O(1) claim is asserted, not proved.
   185	
   186	### CONFIRMED CORRECT
   187	
   188	7. Perron integral representation (Section 1): analytically and computationally verified.
   189	8. Laurent expansion and residue formula (Section 2): verified by independent contour integration.
   190	9. All zeta zero coefficients (Section 3): verified to 5+ significant figures.
   191	10. Phase prediction 5.208 (Section 5): verified to 4 decimal places.
   192	11. Density 1/2 (Section 6): sound argument under GRH + LI.
   193	12. P(T > 0) ~ 0.47 at N = 10^7 (Section 6.3): robust to constant error.
   194	13. Novelty assessment (Section 8.4): honest and accurate.
   195	
   196	---
   197	
   198	## Previous Audit Missed the +2 Error -- Why?
   199	
   200	The first audit (2026-03-30) verified the residue formula Res_{s=0} = -2(logN + gamma) + 2log(2pi) correctly, and verified all c_k and d_k values. But it did not check whether the constant in the EXPLICIT FORMULA (Section 4.1) matched the residue. The explicit formula introduces an extra "+2" that is not present in the residue, and the first audit did not perform the brute-force cross-check that catches this discrepancy. **Lesson: always cross-check the final formula against direct computation, not just the intermediate steps.**
   201

The core mathematical content is sound (conditional on GRH + LI) and the numerical predictions are validated. The honest novelty assessment (Section 8.4) is appreciated.
