/bin/zsh -lc "nl -ba experiments/UNCONDITIONAL_PHASE_LOCK.md | sed -n '500,570p'" in /Users/saar/Desktop/Farey-Local
 succeeded in 0ms:
   500	    T(N) ~ -2*log(N) + C_0 + 2*Re[c_1 * N^{1/2+i*gamma_1}]
   501	           + 2*Re[c_* * N^{beta+i*gamma_1}] + (other zeros) - M(N)
   502	
   503	The new term 2*Re[c_* * N^{beta+i*gamma_1}] has amplitude |c_*| * N^beta,
   504	which for beta > 1/2 grows FASTER than the standard N^{1/2} term.
   505	
   506	### 6.3. Impact on the phase-lock resultant
   507	
   508	The observed phase-lock has:
   509	- Resultant R = 0.77 at phase 5.28 (from 922 M(p)=-3 primes to 10^7).
   510	- Predicted phase (under GRH): 5.208.
   511	- Discrepancy: 0.07 radians.
   512	
   513	**Case: beta = 0.6 (moderately off-line).**
   514	
   515	The off-line zero contributes N^{0.6} vs the on-line N^{0.5}. At N = 10^6:
   516	- On-line amplitude: 0.0971 * 1000 = 97.1
   517	- Off-line amplitude (with |c_*| ~ 0.05): 0.05 * 10^{3.6} = 199.0
   518	
   519	The off-line term would DOMINATE by a factor of 2. The phase of T > 0 would
   520	be controlled by arg(c_*) rather than arg(c_1), shifting the predicted phase.
   521	
   522	Unless arg(c_*) happens to match arg(c_1), the phase-lock would be at a
   523	DIFFERENT angle. The probability of arg(c_*) being within 0.07 radians of
   524	the predicted 5.21 is about 0.07/(2*pi) = 1.1%.
   525	
   526	**Case: beta = 0.55 (slightly off-line).**
   527	
   528	At N = 10^6:
   529	- On-line: 0.0971 * 1000 = 97.1
   530	- Off-line: |c_*| * 10^{3.3} = |c_*| * 1995
   531	
   532	Even at beta = 0.55, the off-line term still dominates at N = 10^6. However,
   533	the dominance ratio is N^{beta-1/2} = N^{0.05}, which is only 3.55 at N = 10^6.
   534	The on-line and off-line terms would be COMPARABLE, creating a more complex
   535	interference pattern.
   536	
   537	### 6.4. What the observed phase-lock tells us
   538	
   539	The key observation: **the predicted phase (5.208) from GRH matches the observed
   540	phase (5.28) to within 0.07 radians.** This match would be destroyed if an
   541	off-line zero with sufficiently different arg(c_*) contributed comparably.
   542	
   543	**Quantitative test.** Define the "GRH mismatch" as:
   544	
   545	    Delta_phase = |observed_phase - GRH_predicted_phase|
   546	
   547	Under GRH: Delta_phase arises from finite-N effects (higher zeros, statistical
   548	fluctuation). Expected order: O(1/R_amplitude) ~ O(1/sqrt(K)) where K is the
   549	number of primes. For K = 922: O(1/30) ~ 0.03. Observed: 0.07. Consistent.
   550	
   551	With an off-line zero at beta = 0.6 and random arg(c_*): Delta_phase would be
   552	uniformly distributed in [0, pi]. The probability of Delta_phase < 0.07 is
   553	about 0.07/pi = 2.2%. So the observed match is UNLIKELY (at 2.2%) if an off-line
   554	zero at beta = 0.6 exists.
   555	
   556	With an off-line zero at beta = 0.55: the interference between on-line and
   557	off-line terms would typically give Delta_phase ~ 0.5-1.0 radians. The probability
   558	of Delta_phase < 0.07 is about 5%.
   559	
   560	**Conclusion:** The observed phase match (0.07 radians) is weak statistical
   561	evidence against off-line zeros with |beta - 1/2| > 0.05 and gamma near gamma_1.
   562	The evidence is NOT a proof and has low statistical power (p-value ~ 2-5%),
   563	but it is in the right direction.
   564	
   565	### 6.5. The resultant as GRH diagnostic
   566	
   567	Under GRH, the resultant R of the phase-lock should be approximately:
   568	
   569	    R_{GRH} = |c_1| / sigma_total
   570

   170	
   171	## 9. Per-Step Discrepancy / Incremental Discrepancy
   172	
   173	### Search Result: NO PRIOR WORK FOUND
   174	
   175	The terms "per-step discrepancy" and "incremental discrepancy" in the context of Farey sequences returned ZERO results across all databases searched. This is strong evidence that:
   176	
   177	**Our per-step Farey discrepancy DeltaW(p) is genuinely novel.**
   178	
   179	No paper studies how the discrepancy changes when transitioning from F_{n-1} to F_n (or when inserting fractions with denominator n). The entire literature focuses on the GLOBAL discrepancy of F_n, not the per-step change.
   180	
   181	### Closest Related Work:
   182	- The BCZ map (Boca-Cobeli-Zaharescu) encodes spacing statistics, which is related but not the same as per-step discrepancy changes.
   183	- Pair correlation measures spacings between adjacent Farey fractions, which is one component of what changes at each step.
   184	
   185	---

  1095	%% SECTION 11: OPEN PROBLEMS
  1096	%% ================================================================
  1097	\section{Open Problems}\label{sec:open}
  1098	
  1099	\begin{enumerate}
  1100	  \item \textbf{Unconditional density bounds.}
  1101	    Prove that the density of $\Delta W > 0$ among $M(p) = -3$ primes
  1102	    lies in some explicit interval $[\varepsilon, 1-\varepsilon]$ with
  1103	    effective~$\varepsilon > 0$.  The current unconditional result gives
  1104	    positive lower logarithmic density but with an ineffective (and
  1105	    presumably tiny) constant.
  1106	
  1107	  \item \textbf{Universal rho stability.}
  1108	    Prove analytically that $\rho \to -3.88\ldots$ as $p \to \infty$.
  1109	    This would reduce the sign question for $\Delta W$ entirely to the
  1110	    behavior of $T(N)$.
  1111	
  1112	  \item \textbf{Cross-control anomaly.}
  1113	    Explain why $T_{\chi_7}$ shows a $19.4\times$ signal at the
  1114	    \emph{zeta} zero when using all primes (but not when restricted to
  1115	    $M(p) = -3$).  Is this genuine cross-talk between $L$-functions,
  1116	    or a sampling artifact?
  1117	
  1118	  \item \textbf{Higher-degree $L$-functions.}
  1119	    Compute $T_f(N)$ for the Ramanujan $\tau$-function and verify
  1120	    phase-lock at zeros of $L(s, \Delta)$.  The Perron mechanism
  1121	    is universal, but the ``M\"obius inverse'' coefficients of a
  1122	    modular form $L$-function have more complex structure.
  1123	
  1124	  \item \textbf{Root number signature.}
  1125	    For characters $\chi$ with root number $\varepsilon(\chi) = -1$
  1126	    (forcing $L(1/2, \chi) = 0$), the zero at $s = 1/2$ contributes
  1127	    a non-oscillatory constant to $T_\chi$ via the Perron residue.
  1128	    Detect this as a persistent bias in $T_\chi(N)$.
  1129	
  1130	  \item \textbf{Anomalous $\gamma_4$ strength.}
  1131	    The fourth zeta zero ($\gamma_4 = 30.43$) shows phase-lock
  1132	    $R = 0.61$, nearly as strong as $\gamma_2$.  Is this
  1133	    second-harmonic coupling ($\gamma_4 \approx 2\gamma_1$), or does
  1134	    it reflect deeper structure in the zero distribution?
  1135	
  1136	  \item \textbf{Stern--Brocot spectral structure.}
  1137	    Does the Stern--Brocot per-level discrepancy connect to the
  1138	    spectrum of the Gauss map transfer operator?  The governing
  1139	    function $?(x)$ arises from the continued fraction algorithm,
  1140	    whose spectral theory involves the Ruelle transfer operator.
  1141	
  1142	  \item \textbf{Arithmetic geometry interpretation.}
  1143	    When fractions $k/p$ enter $\F_p$, they correspond to $p$-torsion
  1144	    points on modular curves.  Does $\Delta W(p)$ have a geometric
  1145	    interpretation as a regularity measure for torsion distribution

   150	The per-step framework yields a chain of results connecting elementary
   151	algebra to the deepest structure in analytic number theory.
   152	
   153	\begin{enumerate}[(1)]
   154	  \item \textbf{Four-term decomposition} (Theorem~\ref{thm:four-term}).
   155	    An exact algebraic identity
   156	    $n'^2 \Delta W(p) = A - B - C - D$,
   157	    decomposing each prime step into four arithmetically interpretable
   158	    terms.  Moreover, $|D/A - 1| = O(1/p^2)$, so the sign of
   159	    $\Delta W(p)$ is controlled by $B + C$.
   160	
   161	  \item \textbf{Regression identity} (Theorem~\ref{thm:regression}).
   162	    $B'/C' = \alpha + \rho$,
   163	    where $\alpha$ is a regression slope connected to the Mertens
   164	    function and $\rho \approx -3.9$ is a stable residual.  This
   165	    is an exact algebraic identity for every prime $p \ge 5$.
   166	
   167	  \item \textbf{Computational Sign Theorem} (Section~\ref{sec:sign}).
   168	    $\Delta W(p) < 0$ for all 4{,}617 primes $p \le 10^5$ with
   169	    $M(p) \le -3$, with zero exceptions.  The first counterexample
   170	    occurs at $p = 243{,}799$, where $T(N)$ crosses zero for the
   171	    first time on this subsequence.
   172	
   173	  \item \textbf{Perron integral for $T(N)$}
   174	    (Theorem~\ref{thm:perron}, conditional on GRH).
   175	    \[
   176	      T(N) = -2\log N + C_0 + \sum_\rho c_\rho N^\rho + O(1),
   177	    \]
   178	    with $c_\rho = \zeta(\rho+1)/(\rho\,\zeta'(\rho))$ and
   179	    $C_0 = 2 - 2\gamma + 2\log 2\pi = 4.521\ldots$
   180	
   181	  \item \textbf{Phase-lock theorem and Chebyshev bias}
   182	    (Theorem~\ref{thm:phase-lock}, conditional on GRH+LI).
   183	    The logarithmic density of $\{p : \Delta W(p) > 0\}$
   184	    among $M(p) = -3$ primes is~$1/2$.  The sign of $\Delta W(p)$
   185	    is controlled by the phase $\gamma_1 \log p \bmod 2\pi$.
   186	    The predicted phase peak is $5.208$; observed: $5.28$
   187	    (error $0.07$ radians, $1.1\%$).
   188	
   189	  \item \textbf{L-function spectral decomposition}
   190	    (Section~\ref{sec:l-function}).
   191	    Per-step Farey discrepancy admits a spectral decomposition indexed
   192	    by Dirichlet characters, where each component oscillates at
   193	    frequencies determined by the corresponding $L$-function's
   194	    nontrivial zeros.
   195	
   196	  \item \textbf{Ordering principle} (Section~\ref{sec:ordering}).
   197	    Different orderings of the same rationals reveal different
   198	    spectral data---or none at all.  The Stern--Brocot tree produces
   199	    monotone exponential growth in $\Delta W$ with ratio $\to 2$
   200	    and zero spectral content.  The Farey ordering by denominator
   201	    is special because it engages multiplicative number theory,
   202	    making zeta zeros visible.
   203	
   204	  \item \textbf{Unconditional oscillation}
   205	    (Theorem~\ref{thm:unconditional}).
   206	    $T(N) = \Omega_\pm(\sqrt{N})$, proved by adapting Ingham's 1942
   207	    method.  Both $\Delta W > 0$ and $\Delta W < 0$ occur infinitely
   208	    often among $M(p) = -3$ primes.
   209	\end{enumerate}
   210

   747	\section{$L$-Function Spectral Decomposition}\label{sec:l-function}
   748	
   749	\subsection{Twisted summatory functions}
   750	
   751	For a Dirichlet character $\chi \bmod q$, define the twisted Mertens
   752	function $M_\chi(x) = \sum_{k \le x} \mu(k)\chi(k)$ and the twisted
   753	summatory function
   754	\begin{equation}\label{eq:T-chi}
   755	  T_\chi(N) = \sum_{m=2}^{N} \frac{M_\chi(\lfloor N/m \rfloor)}{m}.
   756	\end{equation}
   757	When $\chi = \chi_0$ (the trivial character mod~1), this
   758	reduces to the original~$T(N)$.
   759	
   760	\begin{theorem}[Spectral decomposition principle]\label{thm:spectral-decomp}
   761	Per-step Farey discrepancy admits a spectral decomposition indexed by
   762	Dirichlet characters, where each component $T_\chi(N)$ oscillates at
   763	frequencies determined by the nontrivial zeros of~$L(s,\chi)$.
   764	
   765	More precisely, the Perron integral representation
   766	\begin{equation}\label{eq:perron-chi}
   767	  T_\chi(N) + M_\chi(N)
   768	  = \frac{1}{2\pi i}\int_{(c)} N^s \cdot
   769	  \frac{L(s{+}1,\chi)}{s\,L(s,\chi)}\,ds
   770	\end{equation}
   771	has poles at the nontrivial zeros of $L(s,\chi)$, with residues
   772	\begin{equation}\label{eq:residue-chi}
   773	  c_{\rho,\chi} = \frac{L(\rho{+}1,\chi)}{\rho\, L'(\rho,\chi)}
   774	  \cdot N^\rho.
   775	\end{equation}
   776	\end{theorem}
   777

     1	# L-Function Deep Exploration: Spectral Decomposition of Farey Discrepancy
     2	
     3	**Date:** 2026-03-31
     4	**Status:** Computational results complete; theoretical analysis in progress
     5	**Verification Status:** UNVERIFIED -- results require independent replication per protocol
     6	**Classification:** C1 (collaborative, minor novelty) pending expert assessment
     7	
     8	---
     9	
    10	## Background
    11	
    12	We confirmed that twisting Farey discrepancy by Dirichlet characters chi produces phase-locks at zeros of L(s,chi). Three characters were tested in the initial run (trivial/zeta, chi mod 4, chi mod 3), all showing clear phase-lock. This document extends the investigation to seven deeper questions.
    13	
    14	## Question 1: More Characters (mod 5, 7, 8)
    15	
    16	### Setup
    17	
    18	Tested the following new characters:
    19	
    20	| Character | Modulus | Order | Primitive | First zero gamma_1 |
    21	|-----------|---------|-------|-----------|---------------------|
    22	| chi5_quad (Legendre (./5)) | 5 | 2 | Yes | 6.1836 |
    23	| chi5_quartic | 5 | 4 | Yes | 6.6485 |

    28

    32	|------|--------|--------|--------|--------|-----------|---------|
    33	| T_chi5_quad at own zero | 0.133 | 2.8x | 0.000 | 0.0x | 2.8 | MODERATE |
    34	| T_chi5_quartic at own zero | 0.276 | 4.1x | 0.179 | 2.7x | 4.1 | STRONG |
    35	| T_chi7_quad at own zero | 0.287 | 5.0x | 0.601 | 7.3x | 7.3 | STRONG |
    36	| T_chi7_cubic at own zero | 0.143 | 2.1x | 0.144 | 2.2x | 2.2 | MODERATE |
    37	| T_chi8_a at own zero | 0.072 | 1.5x | 0.000 | 0.0x | 1.5 | WEAK |
    38	| T_chi8_b at own zero | 0.130 | 2.6x | 0.184 | 1.1x | 2.6 | MODERATE |
    39	
    40	### Results: ALL primes <= 50,000 (5,133 primes)
    41

    43	|------|-----------|---------|
    44	| T_plain at gamma1(zeta) | 12.9x | STRONG |
    45	| T_chi5_quad at own zero | 10.3x | STRONG |
    46	| T_chi7_quad at own zero | 12.8x | STRONG |
    47	| T_chi8_a at own zero | 12.4x | STRONG |
    48	
    49	### Interpretation
    50	
    51	**Every character tested shows phase-lock at its own L-function zero when using all primes.** The signal strength (10-13x random) is remarkably consistent across characters of different moduli and orders. With the restricted M(p)=-3 sample, signals are weaker and noisier, but still detectable for most characters.
    52	
    53	The quartic (order 4) and cubic (order 3) characters also show phase-lock, confirming this is not limited to quadratic characters. For complex-valued T_chi, the analysis uses |T_chi| thresholding rather than sign, and the signal remains clear.
    54	
    55	**Answer to Q1: YES, all characters produce phase-locks. This includes higher-order (non-quadratic) characters.**
    56	
    57	---
    58	
    59	## Question 2: Non-Primitive Characters (mod 12)
    60	
    61	### Setup
    62	
    63	chi mod 12 has four non-trivial characters. We tested two imprimitive ones:
    64	- chi12_from3: induced from the primitive character chi_3 (conductor 3)
    65	- chi12_from4: induced from the primitive character chi_{-4} (conductor 4)
    66	
    67	If chi mod q is induced from a primitive character chi* mod q*, then L(s, chi) = L(s, chi*) * product_{p|q, p not dividing q*} (1 - chi*(p)/p^s). The zeros of L(s, chi) are exactly the zeros of L(s, chi*) plus possible zeros from the finite product. So we expect T_chi to lock at zeros of the primitive L-function.
    68	
    69	### Results: M(p)=-3 primes
    70	
    71	| Test | max sigma | Verdict |
    72	|------|-----------|---------|
    73	| T_chi12(from3) at gamma1(L,chi_3) | **7.0x** | STRONG |
    74	| CTRL: T_chi12(from3) at gamma1(L,chi_4) | 2.7x | MODERATE |
    75	| T_chi12(from4) at gamma1(L,chi_{-4}) | **4.0x** | STRONG |
    76	| CTRL: T_chi12(from4) at gamma1(L,chi_3) | 4.1x | STRONG (anomalous) |
    77	
    78	### Results: ALL primes
    79	
    80	| Test | max sigma |
    81	|------|-----------|
    82	| T_chi12(from3) at gamma1(L,chi_3) | **20.1x** |
    83	
    84	### Interpretation
    85	
    86	**Non-primitive characters DO lock at zeros of the underlying primitive L-function**, exactly as predicted by the Perron integral formula. The chi12_from3 signal at the chi_3 zero (7.0x with M(p)=-3, 20.1x with all primes) is actually STRONGER than the primitive chi_3 signal from the initial run (6.6x).
    87	
    88	**However, the control for chi12_from4 is anomalously high (4.1x).** This suggests some cross-talk between L-functions, which we analyze further below.
    89	
    90	**Answer to Q2: YES, imprimitive characters lock at zeros of their primitive inducing character. The T_chi formalism correctly factors through the conductor.**
    91	
    92	---
    93	
    94	## Question 3: The Perron Integral for Twisted T_chi
    95	
    96	### Theoretical Derivation
    97	
    98	Define T_chi(N) = sum_{m=2}^{N} M_chi(floor(N/m)) / m, where M_chi(x) = sum_{k<=x} mu(k)*chi(k).
    99	
   100	The Dirichlet series identity is: sum_{n=1}^{infty} mu(n)*chi(n) / n^s = 1/L(s,chi) for Re(s) > 1.
   101	
   102	By Perron's formula (generalized), the partial sums of mu*chi are:
   103	  M_chi(x) = (1/2pi*i) * integral_{c-iT}^{c+iT} x^s / (s * L(s,chi)) ds + error
   104	
   105	The summatory function T_chi(N) then satisfies:
   106	  T_chi(N) = sum_{m=2}^{N} M_chi(N/m) / m
   107	
   108	Substituting the Perron representation and swapping sum and integral (justified for c > 1):
   109	  T_chi(N) = (1/2pi*i) * integral N^s * [sum_{m=2}^{N} 1/(m^{s+1})] / (s * L(s,chi)) ds
   110	
   111	For large N, sum_{m=2}^{N} 1/m^{s+1} approaches zeta(s+1) - 1, so:
   112	  T_chi(N) + M_chi(N) ~ (1/2pi*i) * integral N^s * zeta(s+1) / (s * L(s,chi)) ds
   113	
   114	But more precisely, we can write:
   115	  **T_chi(N) + M_chi(N) = (1/2pi*i) * integral_{c-iInf}^{c+iInf} N^s * L(s+1, chi) / (s * L(s, chi)) ds**
   116	
   117	Wait -- this requires more care. The correct identity:
   118	
   119	sum_{m=1}^{infty} M_chi(x/m) / m = [1/L(s,chi)] * zeta(s) evaluated via Dirichlet series convolution.
   120	Actually, sum_{n=1}^{infty} (sum_{d|n} mu(d)*chi(d)) / n^s = L(s,chi)^{-1} * zeta(s) ... no.
   121	
   122	Let me be precise. Define f(n) = mu(n)*chi(n) and g(n) = 1/n (the function that gives harmonic sums). Then:
   123	  T_chi(N) = sum_{m=2}^{N} (sum_{k<=N/m} f(k)) / m = sum_{n<=N} f(n) * sum_{m=2}^{N/n} 1/m
   124	
   125	The inner sum is H(N/n) - 1 where H(x) is the harmonic number. So:
   126	  T_chi(N) = sum_{n<=N} mu(n)*chi(n) * [H(N/n) - 1]
   127	           = sum_{n<=N} mu(n)*chi(n)*H(N/n) - M_chi(N)
   128	
   129	Therefore: **T_chi(N) + M_chi(N) = sum_{n<=N} mu(n)*chi(n)*H(N/n)**
   130	
   131	Via Perron's formula, this has the generating function:
   132	  sum_{N=1}^{infty} [T_chi(N) + M_chi(N)] / N^s
   133	  = [1/L(s,chi)] * [terms involving zeta'(s)/zeta(s)]
   134	
   135	The poles of this expression occur at zeros of L(s,chi), confirming the phase-lock mechanism.
   136	
   137	### Numerical Verification via FFT
   138	
   139	We computed T_chi5_quad(N) / sqrt(N) for N in [1000, 10000] and took the FFT on a log(N) scale. If T_chi oscillates as N^{1/2} * cos(gamma * log N), the FFT should peak at frequency gamma.
   140

/bin/zsh -lc "nl -ba experiments/UNCONDITIONAL_PHASE_LOCK.md | sed -n '120,180p'" in /Users/saar/Desktop/Farey-Local
 succeeded in 0ms:
   120	    T(N) = Omega_{\pm}(sqrt(N) * exp(-c * sqrt(log log N)))
   121	
   122	for some effective c > 0. This means T(N) achieves values of order sqrt(N)
   123	(up to slowly-decaying factors) with both signs.
   124	
   125	### 1.4. Corollary for DeltaW
   126	
   127	**Corollary 1 (Unconditional).** There exist infinitely many primes p with
   128	M(p) = -3 and DeltaW(p) > 0.
   129	
   130	**Proof.** From T_NEGATIVITY_PROOF.md, DeltaW(p) > 0 occurs when T(N) > threshold
   131	(where N = p-1). Since T(N) = Omega_+(sqrt(N)), there are arbitrarily large N
   132	where T(N) > 0. The primes p with M(p) = -3 have positive density among all
   133	primes (since M(p) = -3 iff mu(p) = -1 and M(p-1) = -2, and by the Erd\"os-Kac
   134	theorem and the oscillation of M, this happens for a positive proportion of primes).
   135	By a sieve argument, the set of N with T(N) > c*sqrt(N) intersects the set of
   136	N = p-1 with M(p) = -3 infinitely often.
   137	
   138	More precisely: the set {N : T(N) > epsilon*sqrt(N)} has positive lower logarithmic
   139	density (by the Omega_+ result), and the set {N : N+1 is prime, M(N+1) = -3} has
   140	positive lower density (unconditionally, since M(N) = -3 for a positive proportion
   141	of integers N, and the primes are well-distributed among these). The intersection
   142	is therefore infinite.    QED.
   143	
   144	**Corollary 2 (Unconditional).** There exist infinitely many primes p with
   145	M(p) = -3 and DeltaW(p) < 0.
   146	
   147	**Proof.** This is immediate from the Sign Theorem: DeltaW(p) < 0 for ALL primes
   148	p in [11, 100,000] with M(p) <= -3 (4,617 primes verified). The infinitude of
   149	such primes is guaranteed since M(x) <= -3 for a positive proportion of integers
   150	x, and prime values in this set are infinite by standard arguments.
   151	
   152	Actually, the Sign Theorem gives more: even without the Omega_- result, we have
   153	an infinite verified set. But the Omega_- result strengthens this to say that
   154	even for LARGE primes (where the oscillation could in principle dominate), there
   155	are infinitely many with DeltaW < 0.    QED.
   156	
   157	---
   158	
   159	## 2. Effective Bounds on T(N) (UNCONDITIONAL, Ineffective Constant)
   160	
   161	### 2.1. The Walfisz bound for M(x)
   162	
   163	**Theorem (Walfisz 1963).** There exists an absolute constant c > 0 such that
   164	
   165	    |M(x)| <= x * exp(-c * (log x)^{3/5} / (log log x)^{1/5})
   166	
   167	for all x >= 2. The constant c is ineffective (derived from Vinogradov's
   168	zero-free region for zeta).
   169	
   170	**Remark.** This bound is sublinear: |M(x)| = o(x). But the constant c is
   171	unknown, so the bound is not numerically useful. The best effective bound is
   172	El Marraki (1995): |M(x)| <= 0.6438*x/log(x) for x > 1.
   173	
   174	### 2.2. Transfer to T(N)
   175	
   176	**Proposition 2 (Unconditional, ineffective).** There exists c' > 0 such that
   177	
   178	    |T(N)| <= N * exp(-c' * (log N)^{3/5} / (log log N)^{1/5})
   179	
   180	for all sufficiently large N.

/bin/zsh -lc "nl -ba experiments/UNCONDITIONAL_PHASE_LOCK.md | sed -n '260,320p'" in /Users/saar/Desktop/Farey-Local
 succeeded in 0ms:
   260	
   261	### 3.1. What we CAN prove
   262	
   263	**Theorem 1 (Unconditional).** Among primes p with M(p) = -3, both
   264	
   265	    {p : DeltaW(p) > 0}  and  {p : DeltaW(p) < 0}
   266	
   267	are infinite sets.
   268	
   269	**Proof.** This is the content of Corollaries 1 and 2 from Section 1.
   270	
   271	**Theorem 2 (Unconditional, weaker density statement).** The lower logarithmic
   272	density of {p : M(p) = -3, DeltaW(p) > 0} among {p : M(p) = -3} is positive.
   273	
   274	**Proof sketch.** By the Omega_+ result, the set {N : T(N) > epsilon*sqrt(N)} has
   275	positive upper logarithmic density (this follows from the standard proof that
   276	Omega results imply positive measure of the "good" set -- see Montgomery,
   277	Ten Lectures on the Interface Between Analytic Number Theory and Harmonic
   278	Analysis, Theorem 15.2). Since T(N) > 0 implies DeltaW(p) > 0 when M(p) = -3
   279	(from the explicit chain T > 0 => alpha < 1 => possible DeltaW > 0, but we need
   280	the precise threshold), and the M(p) = -3 primes are well-distributed, the result
   281	follows.
   282	
   283	The LOWER bound on the density is very weak (something like exp(-c*(log N)^{1/5}))
   284	and far from the conjectured 1/2.
   285	
   286	### 3.2. What we CANNOT prove unconditionally
   287	
   288	**Cannot prove:** The density of DeltaW > 0 primes approaches 1/2.
   289	
   290	**Reason:** The Rubinstein-Sarnak framework requires three ingredients:
   291	1. An explicit formula for T(N) as a sum over zeta zeros -- requires GRH to
   292	   have ALL zeros contribute on Re(s) = 1/2.
   293	2. Linear Independence (LI) of zeta zero ordinates -- unproved even conditionally
   294	   for all zeros, but a standard working hypothesis.
   295	3. The equidistribution of the "random" phases theta_gamma -- follows from LI.
   296	
   297	Without GRH, the explicit formula has contributions from zeros at unknown real
   298	parts, which breaks the symmetry argument that gives density 1/2.
   299	
   300	**Cannot prove:** The density is bounded away from 0 AND 1 with effective bounds.
   301	
   302	**Reason:** The lower density bound from the Omega result is too small to be
   303	useful, and the upper bound (< 1) would require showing that T(N) < 0 for a
   304	positive density of M(p) = -3 primes, which we know computationally but cannot
   305	prove analytically for all large N.
   306	
   307	### 3.3. Summary of density knowledge
   308	
   309	| Statement | Status |
   310	|-----------|--------|
   311	| Both DeltaW > 0 and DeltaW < 0 occur infinitely often | UNCONDITIONAL |
   312	| Density of DeltaW > 0 is in (0, 1) | UNCONDITIONAL (from Omega + computation) |
   313	| Density of DeltaW > 0 is bounded away from 0 | UNCONDITIONAL (positive lower log-density) |
   314	| Density of DeltaW > 0 is bounded away from 1 | UNCONDITIONAL (Sign Theorem gives infinite DeltaW < 0) |
   315	| Density of DeltaW > 0 equals 1/2 | CONDITIONAL (GRH + LI) |
   316	| Effective lower bound on density > 0.01 | NOT PROVED |
   317	| Effective upper bound on density < 0.99 | NOT PROVED (but computationally clear) |
   318	
   319	---
   320

experiments/CLEAN_PROOF_WRITEUP.md:248:**(c)** The finite-$N$ bias is $O(\log N / \sqrt{N})$: for the effective threshold $\delta(N) = (2\log N - C)/\sqrt{N}$, the probability $\Pr(T > 0)$ deviates from $1/2$ by at most $O(\delta(N))$.
experiments/CLEAN_PROOF_WRITEUP.md:258:**(b)** The phase concentration follows from the dominance of the first zero. Writing $c_1 N^{\rho_1} = |c_1|\sqrt{N}\,e^{i(\gamma_1 \log N + \arg c_1)}$, the real part is maximized when $\gamma_1 \log N + \arg c_1 \equiv 0 \pmod{2\pi}$. Since $T(N)$ is dominated by the $M(N/2)/2$ channel (empirically $r = 0.95$), and $M(N/2)$ oscillates with phase $\gamma_1 \log(N/2)$, the $T > 0$ events concentrate at phase $\gamma_1 \log 2 - \arg(d_1) \pmod{2\pi}$.

codex
The `0.07` is coming from `5.2076` versus an observed circular mean reported only as `5.28`. So the size is fine, but the paper should call it “about `0.07` radians” unless it prints the unrounded observed mean.

   208	    for subset_name, mask in [('T>0', T_pos), ('T<=0', T_neg)]:
   209	        ph = phases[mask]
   210	        if len(ph) == 0:
   211	            continue
   212	        # Circular mean and resultant
   213	        C = np.mean(np.cos(ph))
   214	        S = np.mean(np.sin(ph))
   215	        R = np.sqrt(C**2 + S**2)
   216	        theta = np.arctan2(S, C) % (2 * np.pi)
   217	        print(f"  {subset_name}: n={len(ph):4d}, R = {R:.4f}, circular mean = {theta:.4f} rad ({np.degrees(theta):.1f} deg)")

/bin/zsh -lc "nl -ba experiments/CLEAN_PROOF_WRITEUP.md | sed -n '252,310p'" in /Users/saar/Desktop/Farey-Local
 succeeded in 0ms:
   252	$$Y = \sum_{\gamma_k > 0} 2|c_k|\cos(\theta_k + \arg c_k),$$
   253	
   254	where $\theta_k$ are independent uniform on $[0, 2\pi)$ (this is the Rubinstein--Sarnak framework, made rigorous under GRH + LI).
   255	
   256	**(a)** Since each $\cos(\theta_k + \arg c_k)$ is symmetric about zero, the distribution of $Y$ is symmetric about zero. The condition $T(N) > 0$ becomes $Y > (2\log N - C)/\sqrt{N}$, and since the threshold $(2\log N - C)/\sqrt{N} \to 0$ as $N \to \infty$, the logarithmic density of $\{N : T(N) > 0\}$ equals $\Pr(Y > 0) = 1/2$.
   257	
   258	**(b)** The phase concentration follows from the dominance of the first zero. Writing $c_1 N^{\rho_1} = |c_1|\sqrt{N}\,e^{i(\gamma_1 \log N + \arg c_1)}$, the real part is maximized when $\gamma_1 \log N + \arg c_1 \equiv 0 \pmod{2\pi}$. Since $T(N)$ is dominated by the $M(N/2)/2$ channel (empirically $r = 0.95$), and $M(N/2)$ oscillates with phase $\gamma_1 \log(N/2)$, the $T > 0$ events concentrate at phase $\gamma_1 \log 2 - \arg(d_1) \pmod{2\pi}$.
   259	
   260	Numerically: $\arg(d_1) = -1.693$, $\gamma_1 \log 2 = 9.80 \equiv 3.514 \pmod{2\pi}$, giving a predicted peak at $3.514 + 1.693 = 5.208$.
   261	
   262	**(c)** The finite bias follows from standard estimates on the tails of the distribution of $Y$. The characteristic function $\varphi(t) = \prod_k J_0(2|c_k|t)$ (where $J_0$ is the Bessel function) determines the distribution, and for small $\delta > 0$: $\Pr(Y > \delta) = 1/2 - O(\delta/\sigma)$ where $\sigma^2 = \sum_k 2|c_k|^2 = 0.01018$. $\square$
   263	
   264	---
   265	
   266	## Computational Results
   267	
   268	The following results are established by direct computation and are not conditional on any unproven hypothesis.
   269	
   270	### Result A: Sign Theorem for $p \leq 100{,}000$
   271	
   272	**Theorem (computational).** $\Delta W(p) < 0$ for all 4,617 primes $11 \leq p \leq 100{,}000$ with $M(p) \leq -3$.
   273	
   274	*Method:* Exact rational arithmetic computation over all qualifying primes, with zero violations. For each prime, the four-term decomposition was evaluated by enumerating $\mathcal{F}_{p-1}$ via the mediant algorithm and computing $A$, $B$, $C$, $D$ exactly.
   275	
   276	### Result B: First Counterexample
   277	
   278	**Proposition (computational).** The first prime $p$ with $M(p) \leq -3$ and $\Delta W(p) > 0$ is $p = 243{,}799$.
   279	
   280	At this prime:
   281	
   282	| Quantity | Value |
   283	|----------|-------|
   284	| $N = p-1$ | 243,798 |
   285	| $M(N)$ | $-2$ |
   286	| $|\mathcal{F}_N|$ | 18,066,862,385 |
   287	| $T(N)$ | $+0.165$ |
   288	| $\alpha$ | $0.835$ |
   289	| $\rho$ | $-3.887$ |
   290	| $B'/C'$ | $-3.052$ |
   291	| $D/A$ | $0.981$ |
   292	| ${n'}^2 \Delta W$ | $+6.65 \times 10^9$ |
   293	
   294	The failure mechanism: $T(N)$ crosses zero for the first time on the $M(N) = -2$ subsequence, causing $\alpha$ to drop from its typical value of $\sim 12$ (at $p \sim 100{,}000$) to $0.835$. Since $\rho \approx -3.9$ is stable, $\alpha + \rho = -3.05 < -1$, giving $B + C < 0$. The dilution remainder $A - D > 0$ is too small to compensate, yielding $\Delta W > 0$.
   295	
   296	*Verified by:* Two independent programs (streaming Farey computation and targeted merge-walk), with internal consistency checks ($\sum \delta = 0$ per-denominator, $\sum f\delta = C'/2$).
   297	
   298	### Result C: Phase-Lock
   299	
   300	Among the 922 primes $p \leq 10^7$ with $M(p) = -3$:
   301	
   302	| Quantity | Observed | Predicted (Theorem 4) | Error |
   303	|----------|----------|----------------------|-------|
   304	| Circular mean of $\gamma_1 \log p$ for $T > 0$ | 5.28 | 5.208 | 0.07 rad (1.1%) |
   305	| Resultant length $R$ for $T > 0$ | 0.77 | -- | -- |
   306	| Resultant length $R$ for $T < 0$ | 0.13 | -- | -- |
   307	| Fraction with $T > 0$ in $[10^6, 10^7)$ | 0.462 | 0.47 | 0.008 |
   308	
   309	The $T > 0$ primes are concentrated in the phase window $\gamma_1 \log p \in [4.2, 5.8]$ and completely absent from $[0.5, 3.1]$. The resultant length $R = 0.77$ indicates extremely strong phase concentration. The $T < 0$ primes are nearly uniformly distributed ($R = 0.13$).

codex
There’s another issue in the first bullet: the body only gets `A-D = O(p)`, while `A,D = Θ(p^3)`. That does not by itself make sign reduction “exact”; it only suggests that `B+C` is the decisive term once you prove it dominates the residual. The abstract currently collapses that distinction.
exec
/bin/zsh -lc "nl -ba paper/main_revised.tex | sed -n '280,320p'" in /Users/saar/Desktop/Farey-Local
 succeeded in 0ms:
   280	    (Farey symmetry);
   281	  \item $\bar{D} = \frac{1}{|F|}\sum_{f \in F} D(f) = \frac{1}{2}$;
   282	  \item $\alpha = \Cov(D, f)/\Var(f)$, the OLS slope;
   283	  \item $D_{\mathrm{err}}(f) = D(f) - \bar{D} - \alpha(f - \tfrac{1}{2})$,
   284	    the OLS residual;
   285	  \item $B' = 2\sum_{f \in F} D(f)\,\delta(f)$;
   286	  \item $C' = \sum_{f \in F} \delta(f)^2$;
   287	  \item $\rho = 2\sum_{f \in F} D_{\mathrm{err}}(f)\,\delta(f) \,/\, C'$.
   288	\end{itemize}
   289	\end{definition}
   290	
   291	\begin{definition}[The sum $T(N)$]
   292	\begin{equation}\label{eq:T-def}
   293	  T(N) = \sum_{m=2}^{N} \frac{M(\lfloor N/m \rfloor)}{m},
   294	\end{equation}
   295	where $M(x) = \sum_{k \le x} \mu(k)$ is the Mertens function.
   296	\end{definition}
   297	
   298	\subsection{The displacement--shift identity}
   299	
   300	\begin{proposition}[Displacement--shift identity]\label{prop:disp-shift}
   301	For prime~$p$ and $f = a/b \in \F_{p-1}$ with $f \ne 1$:
   302	\[
   303	  D_{\F_p}(f) = D_{\F_{p-1}}(f) + \delta(f).
   304	\]
   305	\end{proposition}
   306	
   307	\begin{proof}
   308	The rank of $f$ in $\F_p$ equals its rank in $\F_{p-1}$ plus the
   309	number of new fractions $k/p < f$, which is $\lfloor pa/b \rfloor$.
   310	Then:
   311	\begin{align*}
   312	  D_{\F_p}(f) &= D_{\F_{p-1}}(f) + \lfloor pa/b \rfloor - (p-1)f \\
   313	  &= D_{\F_{p-1}}(f) + \bigl(pa/b - (pa \bmod b)/b\bigr) - (p-1)(a/b) \\
   314	  &= D_{\F_{p-1}}(f) + \delta(f). \qedhere
   315	\end{align*}
   316	\end{proof}
   317	
   318	\subsection{The four-term decomposition}
   319	
   320	\begin{theorem}[Four-term decomposition]\label{thm:four-term}

Hostile referee verdict: the abstract is not safe as written. `(ii)` is solid, `(iii)` is basically fine, `(vi)` and `(viii)` are not justified, and the rest need tightening.

1. Preamble: `NEEDS-QUALIFICATION`. “Has never been studied” should be “to our knowledge”; the repo only has a literature search saying no prior work was found, not a proof of nonexistence of prior work. “Reveals structure invisible…” is qualitative. See [main_revised.tex#L55](/Users/saar/Desktop/Farey-Local/paper/main_revised.tex#L55) and [LITERATURE_SEARCH_COMPREHENSIVE.md#L173](/Users/saar/Desktop/Farey-Local/experiments/LITERATURE_SEARCH_COMPREHENSIVE.md#L173).

2. `(i) OVERSTATED.` The exact four-term decomposition is real, but “reducing the sign of `\Delta W` to `T(N)`” is not what Theorem 1 proves. The decomposition is at [main_revised.tex#L320](/Users/saar/Desktop/Farey-Local/paper/main_revised.tex#L320); the bridge to `T(N)` comes only later, only on the `M(N)=-2` subsequence, and the paper then suppresses the `O(1/N)` from the alpha formula when writing the sign chain. See [main_revised.tex#L426](/Users/saar/Desktop/Farey-Local/paper/main_revised.tex#L426) and [main_revised.tex#L444](/Users/saar/Desktop/Farey-Local/paper/main_revised.tex#L444).

3. `(ii) VALID.` The regression identity is stated and proved exactly from the permutation lemmas. See [main_revised.tex#L399](/Users/saar/Desktop/Farey-Local/paper/main_revised.tex#L399).

4. `(iii) VALID.` The computational theorem and the computational first counterexample are clearly labeled as such in the body. I would still clarify that this is the first counterexample to the `M(p)\le -3` sign pattern, not the first positive `\Delta W` prime overall. See [main_revised.tex#L464](/Users/saar/Desktop/Farey-Local/paper/main_revised.tex#L464) and [main_revised.tex#L482](/Users/saar/Desktop/Farey-Local/paper/main_revised.tex#L482).

5. `(iv) NEEDS-QUALIFICATION.` The abstract correctly says “conditional on GRH,” but it omits the theorem’s actual restriction to `M(N)=-2`. Also the zero-sum needs a truncation/order convention; as written it reads like an absolutely convergent sum, which it is not. See [main_revised.tex#L547](/Users/saar/Desktop/Farey-Local/paper/main_revised.tex#L547).

6. `(v) OVERSTATED.` The conditional label is present, but the claimed content is stronger than the theorem/proof support. The theorem is about a logarithmic density for `T(N)>0` and a phase concentration statement, not a proved statement that the sign of `\Delta W(p)` is controlled by `\gamma_1 \log p` on primes. Worse, the proof of the phase location explicitly uses an empirical claim, `M(N/2)` dominance with correlation `r=0.95`, so this is not theorem-grade in its current form. The `0.07` radian match is empirical, on the tested `M(p)=-3` sample. See [main_revised.tex#L641](/Users/saar/Desktop/Farey-Local/paper/main_revised.tex#L641), [main_revised.tex#L679](/Users/saar/Desktop/Farey-Local/paper/main_revised.tex#L679), and [main_revised.tex#L694](/Users/saar/Desktop/Farey-Local/paper/main_revised.tex#L694).

7. `(vi) WRONG.` The paper does not actually decompose the original per-step discrepancy into Dirichlet-character components. It defines twisted analogues `T_\chi`, which is a different claim. The theorem also has no proof in the paper, and the internal exploration note says the theory is still “in progress” and “unverified.” There is even an internal inconsistency: the dependency table marks this result as GRH-dependent, while the theorem and abstract do not. See [main_revised.tex#L751](/Users/saar/Desktop/Farey-Local/paper/main_revised.tex#L751), [main_revised.tex#L760](/Users/saar/Desktop/Farey-Local/paper/main_revised.tex#L760), [main_revised.tex#L1182](/Users/saar/Desktop/Farey-Local/paper/main_revised.tex#L1182), [L_FUNCTION_DEEP_EXPLORATION.md#L4](/Users/saar/Desktop/Farey-Local/experiments/L_FUNCTION_DEEP_EXPLORATION.md#L4), and [L_FUNCTION_DEEP_EXPLORATION.md#L117](/Users/saar/Desktop/Farey-Local/experiments/L_FUNCTION_DEEP_EXPLORATION.md#L117).

8. `(vii) OVERSTATED.` This is at best comparative computational evidence. One alternative ordering with no observed spectral signature does not “confirm” that the effect is a property of denominator-ordering and not of the rationals themselves. The body presents interpretation, not a theorem. See [main_revised.tex#L881](/Users/saar/Desktop/Farey-Local/paper/main_revised.tex#L881) and [main_revised.tex#L896](/Users/saar/Desktop/Farey-Local/paper/main_revised.tex#L896).

9. `(viii) WRONG.` `T(N)=\Omega_\pm(\sqrt N)` is an unconditional statement about all integers `N`. It does not by itself yield infinitely many primes with either sign of `\Delta W`. The paper’s corollary jumps from the integer result to a prime subsequence using unsupported claims about the density of `M(p)=-3` primes and vague “sieve” language. See [main_revised.tex#L932](/Users/saar/Desktop/Farey-Local/paper/main_revised.tex#L932), [main_revised.tex#L962](/Users/saar/Desktop/Farey-Local/paper/main_revised.tex#L962), [UNCONDITIONAL_PHASE_LOCK.md#L127](/Users/saar/Desktop/Farey-Local/experiments/UNCONDITIONAL_PHASE_LOCK.md#L127), and [UNCONDITIONAL_PHASE_LOCK.md#L132](/Users/saar/Desktop/Farey-Local/experiments/UNCONDITIONAL_PHASE_LOCK.md#L132).

Checklist:
- `(1)` Perron and phase-lock are marked conditional in the abstract, yes. That part is fine. It is still incomplete because `(iv)` also needs the `M(N)=-2` restriction, and `(v)` also needs an explicit “empirical phase-location heuristic” caveat.
- `(2)` The `0.07` rad figure is numerically fine: it is about `|5.278...-5.2076|≈0.071`. But it should be written as “about `0.07` radians,” and identified as empirical. See [PERRON_INTEGRAL_T.md#L182](/Users/saar/Desktop/Farey-Local/experiments/PERRON_INTEGRAL_T.md#L182) and [DENSITY_PATTERNS.md#L211](/Users/saar/Desktop/Farey-Local/experiments/DENSITY_PATTERNS.md#L211).
- `(3)` The computational versus analytical boundary is not clean enough. `(iii)` is clean; `(v)` mixes theorem and data; `(vi)` is presented as theorem despite unverified theory; `(vii)` is computation dressed up as confirmation; `(viii)` overstates an integer theorem into a prime consequence.
- `(4)` Missing caveats: “to our knowledge” for novelty, `M(N)=-2` restriction in `(iv)`, truncation convention for zero sums, empirical nature of the phase-location claim, sample restriction to `M(p)=-3` primes, lack of proof for `(vi)`, and the unsupported prime-subsequence transfer in `(viii)`.

If you want a publishable abstract, keep `(ii)` and most of `(iii)`, rewrite `(i)`, `(iv)`, `(v)`, `(vii)`, and either delete `(vi)` and `(viii)` or downgrade them sharply.
